v.t: Emperor Huizong accedes to the throne and Cai Jing rises to power
v.s: In the first month, Emperor Zhezong of Song died, and with the support of Empress Dowager Xiang, Prince Duan Zhao Ji ascended the throne as Emperor Huizong of Song. Huizong was passionate about painting and calligraphy but neglected state affairs. Early in his reign, he dismissed Chancellor Han Zhongyan, who opposed the New Policies, and reinstated Cai Jing. Under the pretext of 'continuing the legacy', Cai Jing restored some economic policies of Wang Anshi's New Policies while vigorously excluding Yuanyou faction members. He listed Sima Guang, Su Shi, and over 120 others as 'treacherous officials', inscribing their names on a stone tablet at the Duanli Gate. Cai Jing's rise to power marked the beginning of the dark political era of the late Northern Song, laying the groundwork for the Jingkang Incident over two decades later.

v.t: Liao Emperor Tianzuo ascends the throne and oppression of the Jurchens
v.s: In the first month, Emperor Daozong of Liao, Yelü Hongji, died, and his grandson Yelü Yanxi ascended the throne as Emperor Tianzuo. Tianzuo was debauched and extravagant, addicted to hunting and pleasure, and intensified the oppression of the Jurchen tribes. He demanded that the Jurchens annually tribute Haidongqing (a type of falcon used for hunting swans), northern pearls, and sable fur. The Liao envoys would arbitrarily demand women from Jurchen tribes to accompany them at night, sparking intense resentment among the Jurchens. It was under these circumstances that Wanyan Aguda gradually unified the Jurchen tribes and eventually rose in rebellion against the Liao. Tianzuo's inept rule directly led to the rapid downfall of the Liao dynasty.

v.t: Rise of the European medieval urban commune movement
v.s: From the late 11th century to the early 12th century, as European commerce revived, many cities in northern Italy, Flanders, and northern France gained autonomy through purchase or armed struggle. In 1101, the citizens of Laon in northern France purchased a charter of autonomy from their bishop and lord, Godry, acquiring the right to form a commune. This movement reflected the challenge of the emerging bourgeois class to feudal lordship, promoting the rise of towns and the development of a commercial economy, and laying the foundation for the later birth of universities and the Renaissance.

v.t: Cai Jing becomes chancellor and the 'Chongning Proscription'
v.s: In the fifth month, Emperor Huizong officially appointed Cai Jing as Left Pushe and Minister of Personnel (chancellor). After taking office, Cai Jing immediately launched a nationwide purge of Yuanyou faction members, forbidding their sons and brothers from serving in the capital, and destroying the engraved tablets of poems and essays by Su Shi, Huang Tingjian, and others. He also erected 'Yuanyou Traitors' Stele', listing Sima Guang, Wen Yanbo, Su Shi, and 309 others on a blacklist. At the same time, Cai Jing implemented the Salt Certificate Law, Tea Lead Law, and Equalized Field Tax Law, ostensibly to increase state revenue, but in reality to extort wealth for Huizong's luxurious lifestyle and the construction of the Genyue. Cai Jing's autocratic rule began the countdown to the fall of the Northern Song.

v.t: Establishment of the cloistered government (Insei) by Retired Emperor Shirakawa of Japan
v.s: After becoming a monk, Retired Emperor Shirakawa (1053-1129) continued to hold state power. He established a 'Cloistered Office' (In no chō) at his residence, Hōjūji-dono, employing close associates and northern samurai (originally private guards of the retired emperor) to handle government affairs, forming the 'Insei' system. The retired emperor issued orders directly through 'Inzen', overriding the authority of the Fujiwara regents and the imperial court. This system weakened the power of the Fujiwara regency but provided political space for the rise of the samurai class (especially the Taira and Minamoto clans), eventually leading to the Heiji Rebellion and the Genpei War, bringing the samurai to the center of Japanese politics.

v.t: Flourishing of legal studies at the University of Bologna
v.s: The Italian jurist Irnerius (c. 1050-1130) systematically compiled and annotated Emperor Justinian's Corpus Juris Civilis, rediscovering the essence of Roman law. His lectures attracted students from all over Europe, forming a law school centered at Bologna. Later, students formed 'nations', and professors formed 'colleges', and their combination was recognized as a university by Emperor Frederick I of the Holy Roman Empire in 1158. The University of Bologna is therefore recognized as the 'Mother of European Universities', and the revival of Roman law began here.

v.t: Massive construction of the Genyue by the Northern Song
v.s: To build the royal garden 'Genyue' (Longevity Mountain), Emperor Huizong ordered Cai Jing to establish the Yingfeng Bureau in Suzhou, appointing Zhu Mian to collect rare flowers and strange rocks from the Jiangnan region. These rocks were transported to the capital via the Grand Canal, with every ten boats forming a 'convoy', called the 'Flower and Stone Convoy' (Huashigang). Zhu Mian, to please Huizong, even demolished houses and destroyed bridges to forcibly move giant rocks, causing immense resentment among the southeastern populace, which became the trigger for Fang La's rebellion. The Genyue took over a decade to complete, exhausting the national treasury, and was destroyed when the Jin army captured Kaifeng.

v.t: Formation of Toledo as the center of the European 'Translation Movement'
v.s: After King Alfonso VI of Castile recaptured Toledo from the Muslims in 1085, the city, which had been the capital of the Visigothic Kingdom and a center of Islamic culture, became a bridge between Christian Europe and the Islamic world. Around 1103, Archbishop Raymond (in office from 1126) began to sponsor translation activities, inviting Jewish, Muslim, and Christian scholars to translate a large number of Arabic works into Latin. These works included Aristotle's logic, Ptolemy's astronomy, Avicenna's medicine, and al-Khwarizmi's mathematics. The Toledo Translation Movement directly contributed to the 12th-century Renaissance in Europe, providing the intellectual foundation for scholasticism and the rise of universities.

v.t: Complex relations between the Crusader states and Byzantium
v.s: Bohemond I, Prince of Antioch, returned from the Crusade to Europe, giving speeches across France to publicize the suffering of Christians in the East, and successfully arranged an engagement to the daughter of King Philip I of France. Bohemond's real purpose was to oppose the Byzantine Emperor Alexios I, as according to the 1098 agreement, Antioch should return its territory to Byzantium. In 1104, Byzantine forces under John Doukas recovered Cilicia and Laodicea, forcing Bohemond to return to Italy to organize a new army. The conflict between the Crusader states and Byzantium exposed the fragility of the Latin East.

v.t: Establishment of Venetian maritime hegemony by the commercial republic
v.s: In 1104, Venice completed the conquest of the Dalmatian cities on the northern Adriatic coast, controlling the strategic strait from the Adriatic into the Mediterranean. Venetian Doge Ordelafo Faliero signed a trade treaty with Byzantine Emperor Alexios I, gaining privileges to establish a merchant quarter in Constantinople and reduce tariffs. St. Mark's Basilica in Venice was also significantly expanded that year to house the bronze horses that would be brought back from the Fourth Crusade. The rise of Venice marked the beginning of the Italian maritime republics' dominance over Mediterranean trade.

v.t: Emperor Huizong's patronage of Daoism and the nationalization of the religion
v.s: Emperor Huizong of Song declared himself the 'Teaching Lord Daoist Emperor' and elevated Daoism to the state religion. In 1105, he ordered the establishment of 'Daoist Schools' in all prefectures and counties, included the Daodejing, Zhuangzi, and Liezi in the imperial examination curriculum, and created Daoist officials and ranks, granting generous treatment to Daoist priests. He also ordered the Daoist priest Lin Lingsu to fabricate myths claiming Huizong was the reincarnation of the 'Great Emperor Lord of Eternal Life'. Huizong spared no expense in building Daoist temples, such as the 'Shangqing Baolu Gong' and 'Shenxiao Yuqing Wan Shou Gong' in the capital. This patronage drained the treasury while suppressing Buddhism and intensifying social conflicts.

v.t: The beginning of the 'Flower and Stone Convoy' calamity
v.s: Zhu Mian established the 'Yingfeng Bureau' in Suzhou, specifically responsible for collecting rare flowers and stones for Emperor Huizong. The stones he selected were often several zhang high and weighed thousands of jun, requiring the demolition of bridges, city walls, and even houses to transport. Every ten boats formed a convoy, towed north along the Grand Canal by thousands of laborers. Anyone who showed discontent was punished for 'great disrespect'. The 'Flower and Stone Convoy' not only consumed massive manpower and resources but also led to a sharp decline in agricultural labor in the southeast, leaving fields barren. When Fang La rebelled, one of the rebel slogans was 'kill Zhu Mian'.

v.t: The golden age of Huizong's painting academy and the Hanlin Academy of Painting
v.s: Emperor Huizong greatly elevated the status of painters, placing the Hanlin Academy of Painting directly under his control. He personally guided artists, demanding that painting 'use form to convey spirit', pursuing a delicate and realistic style. Entrance exams for the academy often used ancient poems as prompts, such as 'a remote mountain conceals an ancient temple' or 'fragrant return on a horse stepping on flowers'. In 1105, Huizong commissioned the compilation of the Xuanhe Painting Manual (Xuanhe Huapu), which收录 (included) 6,396 works by 231 painters from the Wei, Jin, to early Song dynasties. The academy produced masters such as Zhang Zeduan (Along the River During the Qingming Festival) and Wang Ximeng (A Thousand Li of Rivers and Mountains), bringing Song dynasty court painting to its zenith in Chinese art history.

v.t: Henry I reunites England and Normandy
v.s: On September 28, 1106, King Henry I of England defeated his elder brother Robert Curthose, Duke of Normandy, at the Battle of Tinchebray, capturing and imprisoning Robert for life. Henry thus reunited England and Normandy, restoring the lands of his father, William the Conqueror. Henry I established a powerful central financial institution, the Exchequer, introduced circuit courts, and issued the Charter of Liberties to limit the power of feudal nobles. He earned the nickname 'Lion of Justice' and laid the foundations for the Plantagenet dynasty.

v.t: Rise of the Mali Empire in West African gold trade
v.s: After the decline of the Ghana Empire, the center of West African gold trade gradually shifted south to the upper Niger River region. The Mandinka chief (of the Keita dynasty, later founder of the Mali Empire) controlled the Bure and Bambuk gold mines and used the convenient waterway of the Niger to transport gold to Timbuktu and Djenné. In 1106, the ruler of Mali began exporting gold to the Almoravid dynasty in Morocco in exchange for salt, cloth, and horses. The influx of West African gold provided a stable source of raw material for gold coinage in the Mediterranean world, indirectly promoting the development of a monetary economy in Europe.

v.t: War between Byzantium and the Seljuk Turks
v.s: Byzantine Emperor Alexios I led an army in 1107 to attack central Anatolia, then under the control of the Seljuk Sultanate. After years of campaigning, he recaptured Nicaea (modern Iznik) and parts of Bithynia, but failed to completely expel the Turks from Asia Minor. Meanwhile, Alexios exploited internal conflicts among the Crusader states to force the Principality of Antioch to recognize Byzantine suzerainty. The Komnenian military revival temporarily stabilized the empire's eastern borders, but Byzantium's power could no longer restore the territories of Justinian's era.

v.t: Song Dynasty sea trade reaches the Indian Ocean
v.s: In 1107, the Quanzhou merchant Li Chong's ship set sail from Mingzhou (modern Ningbo), loaded with porcelain and silk, heading to the Chola kingdom (modern Coromandel Coast of India) in the Indian Ocean. The Song Maritime Trade Bureau required ships to obtain a 'public permit' before departure, detailing the crew list, types of goods, and destination. The opening of this route marked Chinese ships bypassing Arab intermediaries to directly participate in Indian Ocean trade. The Song compass (floating magnetic needle) and compartmentalized shipbuilding technology provided safety for long-distance voyages.

v.t: Louis VI ascends the throne and strengthens French royal power
v.s: In August, Philip I died, and his son Louis VI (nicknamed 'the Fat') succeeded. Louis VI faced challenges from numerous robber barons within the Île-de-France (the area around Paris), who ignored royal authority, plundered merchants, and attacked churches. Louis used military force to eliminate lords of castles such as Montlhéry and Montfort, consolidating the royal domain. He also granted city charters to towns like Paris and Orléans, winning the support of the burgher class. Louis VI's centralization policies expanded Capetian royal power from a small area around Paris to much of northern France, paving the way for his grandson Philip II Augustus to unify France.

v.t: Establishment of the Kilwa Sultanate in East Africa
v.s: Persian prince Ali ibn al-Hassan of Shiraz led a fleet of seven ships to the East African coast, purchasing the island of Kilwa (modern-day coastal Tanzania) and establishing the Kilwa Sultanate. Kilwa was located at the terminus of the Sofala gold trade, controlling the export of gold from the Zimbabwe Plateau via Sofala to Arabia and India. Kilwa minted copper coins that circulated widely along the Indian Ocean coast, and its architecture blended Swahili, Arabic, and Persian styles. The rise of Kilwa marked the beginning of a prosperous trading era for East African coastal city-states.

v.t: Bolesław III unifies Poland and repels the Holy Roman Empire
v.s: Bolesław III (Wrymouth) emerged victorious from civil war with his brother Zbigniew to become the sole Duke of Poland. In August 1109, Holy Roman Emperor Henry V invaded Poland, ostensibly to intervene in Polish internal affairs. Bolesław III defeated the German army in three battles: Głogów, Psie Pole, and Hundsfeld, skillfully using terrain and ambush tactics. After the victory, Bolesław forced Zbigniew into exile. However, in his 1138 testament, he divided Poland into four principalities for his four sons, leading to 200 years of fragmentation.

v.t: European monastic reform and the height of the Cluniac movement
v.s: The Abbey of Cluny spearheaded a reform movement in the 11th century, emphasizing that monasteries should break free from secular feudal control and submit directly to the Pope. In 1109, the abbot of Cluny, Pontius, established dozens of dependent monasteries in southern France, requiring monks to follow the Rule of Saint Benedict, renounce private property, and pray seven times daily. The Cluniac movement enhanced the moral prestige of the Church but also led to later Cistercian reforms due to its increasing opulence. Its most important achievement was strengthening papal authority, supporting Gregory VII's doctrine of papal supremacy.

v.t: Norwegian King Sigurd I's crusade
v.s: In 1107, King Sigurd I Magnusson of Norway set sail from Bergen with sixty ships and five thousand warriors, heading for the Holy Land of Jerusalem. In 1110, on the way, he helped Count Henry of Portugal capture Sintra and Lisbon (still under Muslim control at the time). Upon reaching the Holy Land, Sigurd joined forces with King Baldwin I of Jerusalem to capture the city of Sidon, presenting Baldwin with a ship featuring a golden beak. He was the first European king to personally participate in a crusade, demonstrating the fusion of Norse Viking tradition and Christian holy war ethos.

v.t: The Knights Hospitaller officially recognized by the Pope (confirmed 1113)
v.s: The Hospital of St. John, built by Amalfi merchants in Jerusalem, grew rapidly under the leadership of its warden, Gerard. By 1110, the hospital had four doctors, dozens of nuns, and hundreds of beds, providing free care for all pilgrims. Gerard wrote a rule requiring members to take vows of poverty, chastity, and obedience. In 1113, Pope Paschal II officially issued a bull recognizing the order as independent of any local bishop and directly subordinate to the Pope. Later, the Hospitallers added military functions, becoming one of the three major military orders defending the Crusader states.

v.t: Conflict between Holy Roman Emperor Henry V and the Pope
v.s: In February, Holy Roman Emperor Henry V led his army into Italy, forcing Pope Paschal II to crown him. A dispute broke out in front of St. Peter's Basilica, and Henry V ordered the arrest of the Pope and sixteen cardinals. Paschal II was forced to submit, crowning Henry and promising to cede the right of investiture of bishops to the emperor. However, two months later, Paschal II, with the support of the Abbey of Cluny in France, declared the promise invalid. This 'Investiture Controversy' continued until the Concordat of Worms in 1122.

v.t: Perfection of the Song Maritime Trade Bureau system and flourishing maritime trade
v.s: The Northern Song government established Maritime Trade Bureaus in ports such as Guangzhou, Quanzhou, Mingzhou, Hangzhou, and Mizhou (modern Jiaozhou, Shandong), responsible for managing overseas trade. The bureaus levied a 'tax extraction' (generally one-tenth) on imported goods and conducted 'monopoly purchases' of certain commodities (such as spices and ivory). In 1111, Emperor Huizong ordered that maritime revenue be used for defense expenditures, and the annual income of the bureaus reached 500,000 strings of cash. Chinese merchant ships reached as far as Zanzibar (Cengba) on the east coast of Africa, bringing back ambergris, frankincense, and tortoiseshell.

v.t: Rise of the Albigensian (Cathar) heresy in France
v.s: A large number of Albigensian adherents appeared in the Languedoc region of southern France. Influenced by Manichaeism, they believed in two gods, one good and one evil, with the material world being the creation of the evil god. The Albigensians rejected Catholic sacraments (especially baptism and the Eucharist), denied papal authority, and opposed tithes and the hierarchical structure of the Church. Their teachings attracted townspeople and nobles disaffected by Church corruption. Pope Callixtus II first condemned the Albigensians in 1112, but failed to stop their spread. Later, Pope Innocent III launched the Albigensian Crusade in 1209 to brutally suppress them.

v.t: Death of Henry, Count of Portugal, founder of the Portuguese County
v.s: Henry of Burgundy, Count of Portugal, died. He was the grandson of Robert I, Duke of Burgundy, and received the County of Portugal as a fief after marrying Teresa, illegitimate daughter of King Alfonso VI of León, in 1096. Henry implemented feudalism in his county, encouraging knights from Burgundy and Flanders to immigrate and cultivate the land, and built castles such as Braga and Coimbra. Upon his death, he left the county to his young son Afonso Henriques, with Teresa as regent. Afonso later declared independence and was proclaimed king in 1139, and Portugal was thus separated from the Kingdom of León.

v.t: Construction of Angkor Wat begins
v.s: Khmer King Suryavarman II (r. 1113–1150) began construction of Angkor Wat, the largest religious building in the world, about four kilometers south of Angkor Thom. The temple was dedicated to Vishnu, one of the three principal gods of Hinduism, and the king considered himself the earthly incarnation of Vishnu. The entire complex covers about two square kilometers, with an outer moat 190 meters wide. The central complex consists of three galleries surrounding five lotus-shaped towers, the highest reaching about 65 meters. The outer wall reliefs extend 800 meters, depicting scenes from the Mahabharata and Ramayana as well as battles of the Khmer Empire. Construction took about thirty years and represents the zenith of Khmer civilization.

v.t: Birth of Jin Taizong, Wanyan Wuqimai
v.s: Wanyan Wuqimai (Emperor Taizong of Jin), the second emperor of the Jin dynasty, was born in 1113. He was the fourth younger brother of Jin Taizu (Wanyan Aguda) and succeeded Aguda after his death. During his reign (1123–1135), he completed the conquests of the Liao dynasty and the Northern Song, capturing Emperor Tianzuo of Liao and Emperors Huizong and Qinzong of Song, and moved the Jin capital from Shangjing (modern Acheng, Harbin) to Zhongdu (modern Beijing). He reformed Jurchen old customs, adopted Han-style bureaucratic systems, and laid the foundation for the sinicization of the Jin dynasty.

v.t: Early application of movable type printing in the Song dynasty
v.s: Bi Sheng invented clay movable type printing in the 1040s, but it was not until around 1113 that this technology began to see limited use in private printing workshops in Hangzhou and the capital. Compared to block printing, movable type printing allowed type to be reused, greatly improving efficiency. Although clay type was fragile, it could already be used to print short classics such as the I Ching and the Daodejing. This technology later spread to Europe via the Mongols and the Silk Road, becoming an important driver of the Renaissance and the Reformation.

v.t: Wanyan Aguda rises in rebellion against the Liao
v.s: In September, Jurchen leader Wanyan Aguda assembled 2,500 men from various tribes on the banks of the Lalin River to officially declare rebellion against the Liao. In his oath, Aguda enumerated the crimes of the Liao: yearly demands for tribute of Haidongqing falcons, northern pearls, and sable furs; Liao 'silver envoys' raping women and making endless demands in Jurchen villages; and Emperor Tianzuo's ordering of tribal chiefs to dance at the 'Fish Head Banquet', which Aguda himself defied. Aguda won his first battle at Ningjiangzhou, then at Chuhuadian defeated a Liao army of 100,000 under General Xiao Sian with only 3,700 men. The rise of the Jurchens would completely transform East Asian geopolitics.

v.t: Prelude to the Concordat of Worms between the Holy Roman Empire and the Papacy
v.s: In 1114, Pope Callixtus II convened the Council of Reims, declaring that the investiture of bishops with the staff and ring (symbols of spiritual authority) by secular rulers was forbidden. Holy Roman Emperor Henry V declared that the Pope had no right to interfere in the appointment of bishops within the empire. The conflict intensified, but through diplomatic efforts by Callixtus II, the Concordat of Worms was eventually reached in 1122: bishops were to be elected by the clergy, the emperor could attend the election and grant the bishop secular authority (fiefs), but the spiritual symbols (staff and ring) were to be conferred by the Pope or his representative. This agreement ended the half-century Investiture Controversy.

v.t: Establishment of the Jin dynasty
v.s: On the first day of the first lunar month, Wanyan Aguda proclaimed himself emperor at what is now Acheng District, Harbin, Heilongjiang Province, establishing the Jin dynasty with the era name Shouguo and the dynastic name 'Great Jin' (Da Jin). Aguda reasoned that the character 'Liao' meant 'wrought iron', which rusts, while 'Jin' (gold) never rusts. Early Jin implemented the Meng'an Mouke system: every 300 households formed a Mouke, ten Mouke formed a Meng'an, serving as both a military and a social organization. Meng'an and Mouke households farmed in peacetime and fought in wartime; this system of integrated military and agriculture kept the Jin army highly effective. In only ten years, the Jin conquered the Liao; in twelve years, they conquered the Northern Song, becoming the most powerful military force in East Asia.

v.t: Battle of Hubudagang
v.s: In December, Emperor Tianzuo of Liao, Yelü Yanxi, personally led a massive army (officially claimed 700,000 but actually about 100,000) to battle Wanyan Aguda's force of 20,000 at Hubudagang (near modern Nong'an County, Jilin Province). Aguda used a strategy of concentrating forces to strike the center, leading 3,000 cavalry directly at Tianzuo's encampment. Tianzuo panicked and fled, causing the Liao army to collapse completely. This battle destroyed the core of the Liao army, and Tianzuo could only flee to Yanjing (modern Beijing). The Battle of Hubudagang was the decisive engagement between Liao and Jin, after which the Jin army took the strategic offensive.

v.t: Marriage of Li Qingzhao and Zhao Mingcheng
v.s: The famous female poet Li Qingzhao (1084–1155) married the epigraphist Zhao Mingcheng in the capital. Zhao Mingcheng was the son of Chancellor Zhao Tingzhi. The couple shared common interests, collecting bronze and stone inscriptions and calligraphy and paintings. Li Qingzhao's early ci poems, such as 'Like a Dream' (Changji xiting ri mu), are full of boudoir charm and delicate grace. After the Jingkang Incident, Li fled south; her husband died, and her collection was lost. Her later ci poems turned to melancholy and sorrow, with 'Slow Slow Song' (Xun xun mi mi) becoming a masterpiece. Li also wrote 'Discourse on Ci', proposing that ci is a distinct genre, and holds a unique place in Chinese literary history.

v.t: Danish crusade conquers Estonia
v.s: Danish King Valdemar I led an invasion of Estonia, forcing the local pagans to accept Christian baptism. The Estonians fought back, defeating a Danish fleet near the Gulf of Riga. However, Valdemar, with the help of Bishop Absalon of Roskilde, built a castle at Lindanisse (modern Tallinn) in northern Estonia. This 'Northern Crusade' movement continued throughout the 12th and 13th centuries, with the participation of the Livonian Sword Brothers and the Teutonic Knights, eventually bringing the Baltic region into Christendom but accompanied by brutal repression of the native populations.

v.t: Early use of gunpowder weapons in the Song dynasty
v.s: According to the Wujing Zongyao, the Song army began to use early gunpowder weapons such as 'fire arrows' and 'caltrop fire balls'. Fire arrows had a gunpowder tube tied behind the arrowhead, propelled by the burning powder; caltrop fire balls contained iron caltrops inside and scattered them upon explosion. These weapons were used in the defense against the Jin in the late Northern Song. Song gunpowder technology was transmitted to Europe via Arab merchants, ultimately changing the nature of warfare worldwide.

v.t: University of Bologna obtains legal status
v.s: Holy Roman Emperor Henry V issued the 'Safety Law', granting students of the law school at Bologna immunity from local secular jurisdiction; only the university court or the bishop could try them. This law, later known as the 'Constitution of the University of Bologna', is considered the beginning of Western university autonomy. The University of Bologna focused on Roman law and had about 2,000 students from across Europe. They later organized 'nations' and elected a 'student rector' to manage university affairs, creating the unique 'student university' model.

v.t: King Yejong of Goryeo promotes Confucian education
v.s: King Yejong of Goryeo (r. 1105–1122) established the 'Cheongyeongak' (Pure Mandarin Pavilion) in the capital Gaegyeong (modern Kaesong) as a place for Confucian scholars to lecture and compile books. He ordered the collection of books scattered by war and, following the Song dynasty's civil service examination system, implemented examinations based on Confucian classics. Yejong also sent students to study at the Song Directorate of Education. The Confucianization of the Goryeo dynasty accelerated during this period; although Buddhism remained the state religion, Confucianism began to play a significant role in governance.

v.t: Foundation of the Knights Templar
v.s: French knight Hugues de Payens and eight companions took vows before King Baldwin II of Jerusalem, forming the 'Poor Fellow-Soldiers of Christ and of the Temple of Solomon'. Baldwin II granted them the Al-Aqsa Mosque on the Temple Mount (the site of the original Temple of Solomon) as their headquarters. The order initially had only nine members, tasked with protecting pilgrims traveling from the Mediterranean coast to Jerusalem. In 1129, the Council of Troyes officially recognized the Knights Templar, and the famous abbot Bernard of Clairvaux wrote their rule. The Templars later developed into an international financial organization, inventing traveler's checks and credit services, becoming the earliest bankers in Europe.

v.t: Mathematical achievements of the Song dynasty: precursor to the Shushu Jiuzhang
v.s: The mathematician Jia Xian, in his Huangdi Jiuzhang Suanjing Xicao, proposed 'Jia Xian's triangle' (Pascal's triangle, six centuries before Pascal) and the 'zeng cheng kai fang fa' (method of extracting roots by successive multiplication) for solving higher-order equations. This achievement was a precursor to the peak of Song and Yuan mathematics. Jia Xian's work is lost but was cited by Yang Hui in his Detailed Commentary on the Nine Chapters. Song mathematics was widely applied in astronomy, land surveying, and commercial calculations, demonstrating the high development of ancient Chinese mathematics.

v.t: Outbreak of the Fang La Rebellion
v.s: In October, Fang La of Qingxi County, Muzhou (modern Chun'an, Zhejiang), organized a peasant rebellion using Manichaean (Mingjiao) teachings. Fang La proclaimed himself 'Saint Duke' with the era name Yongle. The immediate cause was the oppressive 'Flower and Stone Convoy' policy: Zhu Mian's underlings ran rampant in the Jiangnan region, demolishing houses and destroying farmland to collect rare flowers and stones for Emperor Huizong. Fang La's manifesto exposed the dark rule of the Song and called for 'equalizing the noble and humble, equalizing wealth and poverty'. The rebel army quickly grew to hundreds of thousands, capturing six prefectures and fifty-two counties, including Hangzhou and Shezhou. In 1121, Emperor Huizong sent the eunuch Tong Guan with 150,000 imperial troops to suppress the rebellion. Fang La was captured and executed by slow slicing (lingchi) in Kaifeng in August. The rebellion and the subsequent massacre resulted in about two million deaths, including both rebels and innocent civilians.

v.t: Creation of the Jurchen script by the Jin dynasty
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Jin, Wanyan Aguda, ordered Wanyan Xiyin to create a Jurchen script. In 1119, Xiyin, based on Chinese and Khitan characters, created the Large Jurchen script (and later a Small Jurchen script). The Jurchen script was used for official documents, but only among the upper echelons of Jurchen nobility; ordinary Jurchen people still spoke their language. In the late Jin, the Jurchen script was gradually replaced by Chinese characters and was largely lost by the Ming dynasty. Surviving inscriptions in Jurchen, such as the Stele of the Victory at Desheng Tuo, are valuable materials for studying Jurchen history.

v.t: Signing of the Alliance on the Sea
v.s: In order to recover the Sixteen Prefectures of Yanyun (ceded to the Khitan Liao by Shi Jingtang in 938), the Northern Song sent envoy Ma Zheng across the sea from Dengzhou (modern Penglai, Shandong) to the Jin, proposing a joint campaign against the Liao. In 1120, Song envoy Zhao Liangsi (formerly Ma Zhi) concluded the 'Alliance on the Sea' with Aguda at the Jin camp: the Jin would attack the Liao Central Capital (Dadingfu, modern Ningcheng, Inner Mongolia); the Song would attack the Liao Southern Capital (Xijin fu, modern Beijing); after the Liao was destroyed, the Sixteen Prefectures would be returned to the Song, and the Song would transfer the annual tribute previously paid to the Liao (100,000 taels of silver and 200,000 bolts of silk) to the Jin. Although seemingly mutually beneficial, the alliance revealed the weakness of the Song military, laying the groundwork for the later Jin invasion of the Song.

v.t: Widespread use of the compass in navigation
v.s: The Song author Zhu Yu, in his Pingzhou Ketan (Conversations from Pingzhou), wrote: 'Navigators recognize directions by the stars at night, by the sun in the day, and by the compass when it is cloudy.' This is the earliest clear record of the use of the compass in maritime history. Song merchant ships were equipped with floating magnetic needles (a magnetized needle floated in water on a piece of rush), used for navigation on overcast days and at night. The compass allowed Chinese ships to move away from coastal routes, crossing the South China Sea and the Indian Ocean directly, reaching as far as East Africa. This technology later reached Europe via the Arabs, ushering in the Age of Discovery.

v.t: Peak of Song dynasty academy education
v.s: Emperor Huizong ordered the establishment of 'academies' (shuyuan) nationwide to supplement official schools. The four great academies were the White Deer Grotto (Lushan, Jiangxi), Yuelu (Changsha, Hunan), Yingtian (Shangqiu, Henan), and Songyang (Dengfeng, Henan). These academies focused on collecting books, lecturing, ancestor worship, and academic debate, inviting Neo-Confucian masters such as Zhu Xi and Lu Jiuyuan to teach. The core of academy education was 'understanding human relations', emphasizing moral cultivation rather than mere examination preparation. Song academies produced many outstanding scholars and set a model for later academies.

v.t: Suppression of the Fang La Rebellion
v.s: In April, Tong Guan's 150,000 Song troops stormed the last rebel stronghold at Bangyuan Cave, capturing Fang La, his wife and children, and his chancellor Fang Fei. In the battle, Song forces killed over 70,000 rebels; subsequent mopping-up operations led to tens of thousands more civilians being executed. In August, Fang La was executed by slow slicing in Kaifeng. Although the rebellion failed, it severely damaged Song control over the southeast, further weakening Song finances and military strength. Only six years later (1127), the Jin army captured Kaifeng, ending the Northern Song. The rebellion also forced Huizong to temporarily dismiss Zhu Mian, though the Flower and Stone Convoy was soon restored.

v.t: Song campaign against Liao fails
v.s: According to the Alliance on the Sea, the Song attacked the Liao Southern Capital (Yanjing). Tong Guan led 150,000 troops, but Liao general Yelü Dashi defeated them twice, at Liangxiang and the Lugou Bridge. Not only did the Song fail to take Yanjing, but the Liao counterattacked as far as Xiongzhou (modern Xiong County, Hebei). Upon hearing the news, Jin Taizu Aguda personally led Jin forces through the Juyong Pass and easily occupied Yanjing. The Jin refused to return Yanjing to the Song as agreed; only after the Song agreed to increase its annual payment by one million strings of 'proxy tax' did the Jin hand over the plundered and sacked city. This failure exposed the decay of the Song military.

v.t: Signing of the Concordat of Worms
v.s: On September 23, Holy Roman Emperor Henry V and Pope Callixtus II signed the Concordat of Worms in the German city of Worms. The concordat stipulated: bishops and abbots were to be freely elected by the clergy, with the emperor allowed to attend the election and adjudicate disputes; after election, the pope was to grant the bishop spiritual authority (staff and ring), while the emperor granted temporal authority (fiefs, taxes, and judicial rights). This agreement ended the nearly 50-year Investiture Controversy, with both emperor and pope making concessions and establishing a division of authority between church and state.

v.t: King Baldwin II of Jerusalem captured
v.s: While touring the northern part of the Principality of Antioch, King Baldwin II of Jerusalem was surprised and captured by a cavalry raid of the Emir of Aleppo, Ilghazi. Baldwin was imprisoned in Aleppo castle for sixteen months. During this time, the regent of Jerusalem (his cousin, Eustace III) could not rule effectively, and the Crusader states fell into disarray. Baldwin was eventually released after paying a ransom of 80,000 dinars and ceding some border castles. This incident exposed the vulnerability of the Crusader defense system.

v.t: Accession of Jin Taizong and outbreak of Song-Jin War
v.s: In August, Jin Taizu Wanyan Aguda died, succeeded by his younger brother Wanyan Wuqimai (Emperor Taizong of Jin). Using the Song's acceptance of Liao defector Zhang Jue (formerly the governor of Pingzhou, who surrendered to Jin then betrayed them for the Song) as a pretext, Taizong launched a massive two-pronged invasion south in October. The eastern Jin army under Wanyan Zongwang (Wolibu) advanced from Pingzhou (modern Lulong, Hebei) toward Yanjing; the western army under Wanyan Zonghan (Nianhan) advanced from Datong toward Taiyuan. The eastern Jin quickly took Yanjing, then captured Zhending and Xingtai, marching on Kaifeng. Emperor Huizong panicked, abdicated in favor of his son Zhao Huan (Emperor Qinzong), and fled south. The Song-Jin War had begun.

v.t: Northern Song chancellor Zhang Bangchang forced to proclaim himself emperor
v.s: During the first Jin siege of Kaifeng, Emperor Taizong of Jin demanded that the Song cede the three prefectures of Taiyuan, Zhongshan, and Hejian, and send a chancellor to negotiate at the Jin camp. Emperor Qinzong sent Zhang Bangchang and Prince Kang Zhao Gou (later Emperor Gaozong of Song). The Jin took Zhao Gou hostage and forced Zhang Bangchang to accept the title of 'Great Chu' emperor in 1127 as a puppet ruler during the second Jin invasion. Zhang reigned for 33 days before returning authority to Zhao Gou, but Emperor Gaozong nevertheless forced him to commit suicide for 'usurpation'. Zhang's death reflects the harsh purge of pro-Jin officials in the early Southern Song.

v.t: David IV unifies Georgia
v.s: King David IV of Georgia ('the Builder', r. 1089–1125) crushed a Seljuk Turkic coalition (about 100,000 troops) with his 55,000-strong army at the Battle of Didgori. David used a tactic of luring the enemy in, channeling the Seljuk cavalry into the narrow Didgori valley, then launching surprise attacks from the high ground on both flanks. After the battle, Georgia recaptured Tbilisi, which had been occupied by the Seljuks for four centuries, and moved the capital back from Kutaisi to Tbilisi. David also carried out military reforms, creating a standing army of 30,000 cavalry and 50,000 infantry, making Georgia the strongest Christian kingdom in the Caucasus.

v.t: Yelü Dashi migrates west and establishes the Western Liao
v.s: On the eve of the Liao dynasty's collapse, imperial clansman Yelü Dashi (an eighth-generation descendant of Liao Taizu Yelü Abaoji) fled west with 200 cavalry and some Khitan tribesmen. He crossed the Altai Mountains and reached the city of Khocho (modern west Mongolia), where he gathered local Khitan and Turkic tribes, building a 10,000-man army. In 1132, Yelü Dashi proclaimed himself emperor at Yemili (modern Emin, Xinjiang), establishing the Western Liao (Qara Khitai) with its capital at Balasagun (near modern Tokmok, Kyrgyzstan). The Western Liao ruled Central Asia for nearly a century until conquered by the Mongols in 1218.

v.t: Fall of the Liao dynasty
v.s: In February, Liao Emperor Tianzuo Yelü Yanxi was captured near Yingzhou (modern Ying County, Shanxi) by Jin general Wanyan Loushi, marking the official end of the Liao dynasty. Tianzuo had reigned for 24 years, a period of corruption and the rise of the Jurchens, leading to the dynasty's collapse. The Liao dynasty had lasted 219 years (907–1125), one of the longest-lasting non-Han dynasties in northern China. Tianzuo was taken to Shangjing (modern Acheng, Harbin), where Jin Taizong gave him the title 'Prince of Haibin'. Tianzuo died in captivity, deeply depressed. The fall of the Liao made the Jin the dominant power in the north, directly threatening the Northern Song.

v.t: First Jin invasion of Song ends
v.s: After capturing Yanjing, Wanyan Zongwang led the eastern Jin army south, advancing on Kaifeng. At the urging of Li Gang, Emperor Qinzong decided to defend the capital and called for reinforcements. Under Li Gang's organization, the defenders repelled multiple Jin attacks. Meanwhile, the western Jin army was bogged down at Taiyuan by the stubborn defense of Wang Bing and could not link up with the eastern force. Unable to capture Kaifeng, the Jin concluded an agreement with the Song in December 1125: the Song ceded the three prefectures of Taiyuan, Zhongshan, and Hejian, and paid an indemnity of 5 million taels of gold and 50 million taels of silver. After the Jin withdrew, Qinzong reneged on the territorial cession, setting the stage for a second Jin invasion in 1126.

v.t: Jingkang Incident — Fall of Kaifeng
v.s: In August, Jin Taizong, citing the Song's failure to cede the three prefectures, launched another two-pronged invasion. Wanyan Zonghan's western Jin army captured Taiyuan (defender Wang Bing held out for nine months before falling), while Wanyan Zongwang's eastern Jin army crossed the Yellow River and reached Kaifeng. On November 25, the Jin breached the outer city. Emperor Qinzong went personally to the Jin camp to sue for peace; the Jin demanded 1 million ingots of gold and 5 million ingots of silver. Qinzong ordered the whole city ransacked for gold and silver, even seizing women's hairpins and jewelry, but could not meet the demand. On January 9, 1127, the Jin broke into the inner city, capturing Emperors Huizong and Qinzong, along with consorts, princes, princesses, ministers, and over 3,000 others. The Northern Song had fallen.

v.t: Li Gang defends Kaifeng
v.s: During the first defense of Kaifeng, Vice Director of the Right Li Gang was appointed Commissioner of the Imperial Expedition. He recruited militia, reinforced defenses, and produced thousands of stone catapults and mounted crossbows. When the Jin set up scaling ladders, Li Gang commanded the defenders to counterattack with fire oil, rolling stones, and divine-arm bows, killing over a thousand Jin soldiers. Li Gang also organized night raids on the Jin camp and persuaded Qinzong to execute the eunuch commander who had fled the battle. However, Qinzong, egged on by capitulationist chancellors Bai Shizhong and Li Bangyan, dismissed Li Gang, provoking the anger of the populace. Qinzong was forced to reappoint Li Gang, but during the second Jin invasion, he was sent to Hebei, leaving Kaifeng without its finest defender.

v.t: Birth of Gothic architecture in Europe
v.s: In 1126, Abbot Suger (1081–1151) of the Abbey of Saint-Denis in France began rebuilding the abbey church. He employed three innovative techniques: the pointed arch (replacing the round arch), rib vaulting (concentrating weight on columns), and flying buttresses (external supports for the walls). These structural innovations allowed churches to be built higher and with larger windows, and replaced walls with stained glass. The choir of Saint-Denis was completed in 1144 and is considered the first Gothic building. Gothic architecture later spread across France, with Notre-Dame de Paris, Chartres Cathedral, and Reims Cathedral as its masterpieces.

v.t: Establishment of the Southern Song
v.s: On May 1, the ninth son of Emperor Huizong, Prince Kang Zhao Gou, formally ascended the throne at Nanjing Yingtianfu (modern Shangqiu, Henan) as Emperor Gaozong of Song, changing the era name to Jianyan. Gaozong posthumously honored his captured father Huizong as 'Abyssal Saint Emperor' and his brother Qinzong as 'Filial Piety Abyssal Saint Emperor'. In the early days of the Southern Song, the Jin pursued Gaozong relentlessly; he fled from Yangzhou to Zhenjiang, then to Hangzhou, then to Ningbo, and in 1129 escaped into the East China Sea, drifting among the Zhoushan Islands for months. Only after establishing the capital at Lin'an (modern Hangzhou) in 1132 did the Southern Song government stabilize. The Southern Song lasted 152 years; its economy and culture flourished even more than the Northern Song, but it remained under constant threat from the Jin in the north and later the Mongols.

v.t: Emperors Huizong and Qinzong of Song escorted to the Jin
v.s: In the first month, the Jin escorted Huizong, Qinzong, their consorts, clan members, and over 3,000 others north. The train included Song regalia, ceremonial objects, astronomical instruments, maps, books, and incalculable gold and silver treasures. After arriving at the Jin capital Shangjing (modern Acheng, Harbin), the two former emperors were forced to wear mourning clothes and perform the 'sheep-leading ceremony' (bare-chested, wearing sheep skins as a sign of submission). They were then imprisoned at Wuguocheng (modern Yilan, Heilongjiang). While captive, Huizong wrote poems expressing his sorrow. Huizong died at Wuguocheng in 1135; Qinzong died at Yanjing in 1156.

v.t: Jin establishes puppet Chu and Qi regimes in the Central Plains
v.s: To consolidate its rule over the Central Plains, the Jin set up two puppet regimes. In 1127, the Jin enthroned Zhang Bangchang as emperor of 'Great Chu', but Zhang ruled for only 33 days before returning authority to Zhao Gou. In 1130, the Jin made Liu Yu emperor of 'Great Qi', with its capital at Damingfu (later moved to Kaifeng). Qi served as a buffer state between the Jin and the Southern Song, and frequently joined Jin campaigns against the Song. Qi's brutal misrule caused widespread suffering, and it was finally abolished by the Jin in 1137. The Jin then appointed Wanyan Zongbi (Wuzhu) as governor of Henan province, directly ruling the Central Plains.

v.t: Zong Ze defends Kaifeng but dies unfulfilled
v.s: Emperor Gaozong appointed Zong Ze as defender of the Eastern Capital (Kaifeng), responsible for its defense. Zong Ze arrived, restored discipline, and recruited rebel leaders such as Wang Shan and Yang Jin, building a force of hundreds of thousands. He repeatedly wrote to Gaozong urging him to return to Kaifeng and organize a northern campaign, but Gaozong, obstructed by capitulationist ministers Huang Qianshan and Wang Boyan, never dared to return. Zong Ze fell ill from grief and anger in July 1128, and on his deathbed shouted three times: 'Cross the river!' After his death, Kaifeng again fell into chaos and was soon captured by the Jin.

v.t: Jin's 'Search the Mountains and Scour the Seas' pursuit of Zhao Gou
v.s: Jin Taizong sent Wanyan Zongbi (Wuzhu) south to pursue Emperor Gaozong. In the first month of 1128, the Jin captured Yangzhou, and Gaozong fled hastily to Zhenjiang, crossing south of the Yangtze. In October, the Jin took Jiankang (modern Nanjing); Gaozong fled from Hangzhou to Yuezhou (Shaoxing) to Mingzhou (Ningbo) and then into the East China Sea. Zongbi pursued by sea for months, but typhoons and unrest in his rear forced him to withdraw north in 1130. Han Shizhong blocked the Jin at Huangtiandang for 48 days, inflicting losses. Although this 'search and scour' failed to capture Gaozong, it instilled in him a fear of the Jin that influenced his attitude toward resistance.

v.t: Miao-Liu Rebellion — political crisis in the early Southern Song
v.s: In March, Palace Corps Commanders Miao Fu and Liu Zhengyan, resentful of Emperor Gaozong's favor toward the eunuch Kang Lü and the mediocre Chancellor Wang Yuan (who had been promoted for protecting Gaozong at sea), launched a coup in Hangzhou. They killed Kang Lü and Wang Yuan, forced Gaozong to abdicate in favor of his three-year-old son Zhao Fu, and invited Empress Dowager Meng (Zhezong's empress) to act as regent. Miao and Liu claimed control of the government but could not consolidate power. Generals Han Shizhong and Zhang Jun marched on the capital, defeated Miao and Liu, and restored Gaozong. Miao Fu and Liu Zhengyan were executed. The rebellion exposed the fragility of central authority in the early Southern Song.

v.t: Yue Fei forms the Yue Family Army
v.s: Yue Fei served as a commander under Zong Ze. After Zong's death, he stationed his troops in Yixing and began building his own army, the 'Yue Family Army' (Yue Jia Jun). The army was known for strict discipline, with the slogan 'Rather freeze than tear down a house, rather starve than loot'. Yue Fei incorporated many northern refugees and former rebels (such as Yang Zaixing and Niu Gao) and trained them rigorously, making his army the elite of the Southern Song. The Yue Family Army eventually numbered 100,000, divided into twelve corps (such as the Beiwei Corps and Youyi Corps), with the Beiwei Corps being the most elite, specializing in cavalry charges.

v.t: Increased fragmentation of Kievan Rus'
v.s: After the death of Grand Prince Mstislav I of Kiev, the political unity of Kievan Rus' disintegrated. By 1129, principalities such as Novgorod, Vladimir-Suzdal, and Chernigov had become effectively independent, and the authority of the Grand Prince of Kiev extended only to the immediate vicinity of Kiev. The Rus' principalities fought among themselves, creating conditions for later Mongol invasion. That year, Yuri Dolgoruky of Suzdal (later the founder of Moscow) began large-scale construction, including the first wooden fortress.

v.t: Battle of Huangtiandang
v.s: In spring, when Wanyan Zongbi's Jin army withdrew from Lin'an north, it was intercepted on the Yangtze River at Zhenjiang by Han Shizhong's naval force. With only 8,000 sailors (some say 3,000) and a few dozen ships, Han blocked the 100,000-strong Jin army for 48 days. Han's wife, Liang Hongyu, personally beat war drums to boost morale. Zongbi offered a reward for a solution, and a local suggested using fire ships to burn the Song fleet and digging out the old Laogu River channel to bypass Huangtiandang. After breaking out, Zongbi's army had lost half its strength. The Battle of Huangtiandang shattered the myth of Jin invincibility and is a classic engagement of the Song-Jin War.

v.t: Battle of Fuping and the loss of Shaanxi
v.s: In September, Southern Song Sichuan-Shaanxi Pacification Commissioner Zhang Jun assembled about 180,000 Song troops from five Shaanxi circuits (actually about 100,000 combat troops) at Fuping (modern Fuping, Shaanxi) to face the Jin army of Wanyan Zongbi and Wanyan Loushi, numbering about 50,000. The Song forces were numerous but lacked unified command, with each commander pursuing his own agenda; the Jin, though fewer, were elite. Wanyan Loushi used the mobility of his cavalry to concentrate attacks on the Song's weak point: Zhao Zhe's Huanqing Army. Zhao Zhe panicked and fled, causing the entire Song line to collapse. After Fuping, the Song lost almost all of Shaanxi, and the Sichuan-Shaanxi defense line shrank to the Dasanguan Pass.

v.t: Zhong Xiang and Yang Yao rebellion
v.s: The Dongting Lake region saw a rebellion led by Zhong Xiang and Yang Yao, organized along Manichaean lines. They proclaimed the slogan 'Equalize the noble and humble, equalize wealth and poverty', declaring 'Laws that divide by noble and humble are not good laws; my law will equalize the noble and humble and equalize wealth and poverty.' The rebels captured Dingzhou and Lizhou and established a government. After Zhong Xiang was killed, Yang Yao continued the rebellion, using the lake's inlets and reeds to evade Song forces, employing a guerrilla strategy of 'farming on land and fighting on water'. In 1135, Yue Fei suppressed the rebellion, using a mix of force and offers of amnesty to win over Yang Yao's subordinates Huang Zuo and Yang Qin, then finally broke Yang's fortified water camp. Tens of thousands of rebels and civilians died.

v.t: Battle of Heyuanyuan — Wu Jie defeats Jin Wuzhu
v.s: In October, Jin commander Wanyan Zongbi led 100,000 troops to attack the strategic Heyuanyuan Pass (southwest of modern Baoji, Shaanxi). Song general Wu Jie defended the pass with only a few thousand men. Wu exploited the narrow terrain, deploying crossbowmen in relays and using triple-bow crossbows to fire bolts that could penetrate Jin heavy armor. Zongbi personally supervised the assault, but multiple charges were repelled. Wu also sent troops to raid the Jin camp at night, causing heavy casualties. Zongbi was hit by two arrows and fled after cutting off his beard. The Battle of Heyuanyuan was the first major defeat for the Jin since their invasion, greatly boosting the morale of the Song forces in the Sichuan-Shaanxi region.

v.t: Predecessor of the University of Paris — flourishing of the Notre-Dame cathedral school
v.s: The cathedral school attached to Notre-Dame de Paris reached its peak under the lectures of the philosopher Peter Abelard (1079–1142). Abelard, a leading scholastic philosopher, used logic and dialectic to study theology. His work Sic et Non (Yes and No) collected 156 theological questions, each with patristic quotations on both sides. His lectures attracted thousands of students from across Europe, making Paris a center of learning. Abelard is also famous for his love affair with Héloïse and his conflicts with conservative churchmen. After 1150, the cathedral school developed into a university, receiving a charter from King Philip II in 1200.

v.t: Southern Song establishes capital at Lin'an
v.s: Emperor Gaozong formally designated Lin'an Prefecture (modern Hangzhou) as the 'temporary capital' (xingzai suo). Lin'an is located on the Qiantang River, with Phoenix Mountain to the south and canals connecting to the Jiangnan water network, facilitating transportation. Gaozong rebuilt the central government institutions, the imperial ancestral temple, and the imperial palace. Lin'an rapidly grew from a prefecture into a metropolis of one million people, with merchants gathering, taverns and teahouses everywhere, and West Lake becoming a favorite subject of poets. Although the Southern Song rulers were content to remain south of the Yangtze, the prosperity of Lin'an far surpassed that of the Northern Song capital, Kaifeng.

v.t: Flourishing of the Maritime Silk Road — ports of Guangzhou and Quanzhou
v.s: To increase revenue, the Southern Song government actively encouraged overseas trade. The three major ports of Guangzhou, Quanzhou, and Mingzhou established Maritime Trade Bureaus, imposing a 'tax extraction' on imports. The annual revenue from maritime trade reached up to 2 million strings, accounting for one-fifth of total fiscal revenue. Quanzhou became one of the world's largest ports in the late Song and early Yuan dynasties, exporting porcelain, silk, lacquerware, and copper coins, and importing spices, ivory, pearls, and medicinal ingredients. Quanzhou was home to Arab, Persian, Indian, and European merchants, with mosques and Christian churches. Known as 'Zayton' (for its many capok trees), Quanzhou is described in detail in Marco Polo's Travels.

v.t: Yue Fei recovers the six prefectures of Xiang-Han
v.s: Yue Fei led the Yue Family Army north from Ezhou (modern Wuchang), first capturing Yingzhou (modern Zhongxiang), then dividing his forces to take Suizhou and Xiangyang. Yue himself led the main force to face the Qi general Li Cheng at Xiangyang. Li Cheng had deployed his cavalry along the riverbank and infantry on the plain. Yue spotted the tactical error (cavalry unsuited to the narrow riverbank) and ordered Wang Gui's long-spear infantry to attack Li's cavalry while Zhang Xian's cavalry charged the infantry. Li's army collapsed, and Xiangyang was recaptured. The Yue army then took Dengzhou, Tangzhou, and Xinyang Army, recovering the six prefectures. This was the first large-scale recovery of lost territory since the Southern Song's founding.

v.t: First foundation of Bourges Cathedral
v.s: Construction began on Bourges Cathedral in France. The cathedral is built in the Gothic style, featuring five radiating chapels and double ambulatories, forming a unique 'double-ambulatory, five-chapel' plan. The cathedral contains a large number of stained glass windows depicting biblical stories and Christian saints. Bourges Cathedral is one of the masterpieces of French Gothic architecture and was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1992.

v.t: Song-Jin see-saw battles before the Treaty of Shaoxing
v.s: The main Jin army under Wanyan Zongbi invaded south again, attacking the Huai River region. Song general Han Shizhong set an ambush at Dayizhen (northwest of modern Yangzhou, Jiangsu), defeating the Jin vanguard and capturing the Jin general Tabuye. Yue Fei also led his army north, capturing Yingchang (modern Xuchang, Henan) and Chenzhou. In the Sichuan-Shaanxi theater, however, the Jin captured Xianrenguan and Fengzhou, further shrinking the Song defense line. Neither side could destroy the other, setting the stage for the Treaty of Shaoxing in 1141.

v.t: Death of Henry I of England and the succession crisis
v.s: On December 1, King Henry I of England died in Normandy. Henry's only legitimate child was his daughter Matilda (widow of Holy Roman Emperor Henry V), but the English nobles refused to accept a female ruler. Henry's nephew Stephen of Blois crossed to England and was crowned king at Winchester. Matilda and her son Henry (later Henry II) raised armies to contest the throne, plunging England into a 19-year civil war known as 'the Anarchy'. This conflict weakened the English crown, allowing the church and feudal nobles to expand their power.

v.t: Death of Jin Taizong and accession of Jin Xizong
v.s: On February 9, Emperor Taizong of Jin, Wanyan Wuqimai, died at age 60. During his reign, he completed the conquests of the Liao dynasty and the Northern Song, capturing Emperor Tianzuo of Liao and Emperors Huizong and Qinzong of Song. He reformed Jurchen old customs, adopted Han-style bureaucracy, and moved the capital from Shangjing to Zhongdu. Taizong was succeeded by his nephew Wanyan Dan, known as Emperor Xizong of Jin. Xizong was deeply influenced by Han culture, proficient in Confucianism; he abolished the old Jurchen 'bogda' system (noble council) and fully implemented the Song's three departments and six ministries system, accelerating the sinicization of the Jin dynasty.

v.t: European literature — Troubadour lyric poetry
v.s: The 'troubadours' of the Provence region in southern France created lyric poetry on the theme of 'courtly love'. The most famous poet was William IX, Duke of Aquitaine (1071–1127), but the art form reached its peak around 1135 with poets such as Bernart de Ventadorn and Jaufré Rudel. The poems typically describe a knight's love for a noble lady, emphasizing elegance, loyalty, and restraint. Courtly love poetry broke the monopoly of early medieval religious literature, reflecting the rise of secular culture during the 12th-century Renaissance.

v.t: Yue Fei's second northern expedition
v.s: Yue Fei led the Yue Family Army from Xiangyang on a second northern campaign. He captured Guozhou (modern Lingbao, Henan) and Shangzhou, reaching the Yellow River. Vanguard units of the Yue army reached the outskirts of Luoyang and defeated Jin forces at Yiyang (modern Song County, Henan). Yue's deputy Niu Gao repeatedly defeated Jin forces in the Jingxi region. However, Emperor Gaozong feared that Yue might be cut off from supply lines and ordered him to return to Ezhou. Yue had to abandon the recovered territories and retreat to Xiangyang. Although the campaign failed to recover the Central Plains, it demonstrated the fighting power of the Yue Family Army.

v.t: Owain Gwynedd unifies Gwynedd in Wales
v.s: In 1136, Owain Gwynedd (Owain the Great, 1100–1170), King of Gwynedd in Wales, conquered Powys and Ceredigion, becoming the de facto ruler of most of Wales. Owain allied with King Stephen of England and carried out military reforms, forming a longbow corps (the Welsh longbow later became a decisive weapon for the English in the Hundred Years' War). He also built a series of stone castles to consolidate control over northern Wales. Owain's reign laid the foundations for Welsh national consciousness.

v.t: Jin abolishes Qi, directly rules Central Plains
v.s: In November, Emperor Xizong of Jin issued a decree abolishing the puppet Qi emperor Liu Yu, incorporating the Qi territories (Henan and Shaanxi) directly into the Jin realm. Liu Yu was deposed as a commoner and exiled to Linhuangfu (modern Bairin Left Banner, Inner Mongolia). The Jin established a Branch Secretariat in Kaifeng, administered by Wanyan Zongbi (Wuzhu) and Wanyan Zonghan. Direct Jin rule over the Central Plains, with its harsh policies (such as forced shaving of heads and changes of clothing, land confiscation, and conscription), sparked resistance among the Han Chinese population. The Southern Song took the opportunity to order Yue Fei to recover the six prefectures of Xiangyang.

v.t: Louis VII becomes King of France
v.s: On August 1, King Louis VI of France died, and his son Louis VII (the Young) succeeded. Louis VII was a devout Catholic; he married Eleanor, Duchess of Aquitaine, in 1136, bringing the wealthy Duchy of Aquitaine into the royal domain. Louis VII participated in the Second Crusade (1147–1149) but his relationship with Eleanor soured during the journey. In 1152, Louis had the marriage annulled on grounds of consanguinity and Eleanor's failure to bear a male heir. Eleanor then married Henry II of England, bringing Aquitaine as her dowry into the English crown, which became one of the root causes of the Hundred Years' War.

v.t: Southern Song finalizes capital at Lin'an
v.s: Emperor Gaozong formally declared Lin'an Prefecture the temporary capital and ordered the construction of the imperial ancestral temple, the altars of soil and grain, and the imperial palace. Lin'an became the political, economic, and cultural center of the Southern Song. The 'Great Inner' (imperial palace) was built on the slopes of Phoenix Mountain; though much smaller than the Northern Song capital Kaifeng, it was compact and nestled against the mountain. The Southern Song government established the Directorate of Education, the Secretariat, and the Hanlin Academy in Lin'an. The decision to make Lin'an the capital signaled that the Southern Song regime had abandoned any intention of recovering the Central Plains, turning instead to a peaceful existence south of the Yangtze.

v.t: Coronation of Conrad III of the Hohenstaufen dynasty
v.s: On March 7, Conrad III was crowned Holy Roman Emperor at Aachen Cathedral. He was the first emperor of the Hohenstaufen dynasty. Conrad III engaged in a long struggle with the Welf dynasty of Bavaria, weakening imperial power. He participated in the Second Crusade (1147), but was defeated by the Seljuk Turks in Asia Minor, reaching the Holy Land with only a small remnant of his army. Conrad III's reign marked the decline of the Holy Roman Emperor's power and the intensification of feudal fragmentation.

v.t: First Song-Jin peace agreement (prelude to Treaty of Shaoxing)
v.s: The Jin returned the territories of Henan and Shaanxi to the Southern Song on condition that the Song become a vassal state and pay an annual tribute of 250,000 taels of silver and 250,000 bolts of silk. Emperor Gaozong accepted the terms and ordered generals such as Yue Fei not to take possession of the returned lands. Yue Fei submitted a memorial opposing the terms, saying 'The Jin cannot be trusted, and peace cannot be relied upon,' but Gaozong ignored him. The returned lands were actually a concession by Emperor Xizong of Jin to weaken the influence of hawkish generals such as Wanyan Zongbi. Internal strife between doves and hawks in the Jin court led to the hawks regaining power, and the Song soon lost these territories again.

v.t: Second Lateran Council
v.s: Pope Innocent II convened the Second Lateran Council at the Lateran Palace in Rome. The council declared an end to the schism between Innocent II and the antipope Anacletus II, confirming Innocent's legitimacy. It also issued several decrees: forbidding clerical marriage (enforcing celibacy), forbidding clergy from holding secular offices (such as fiefs, judgeships), and forbidding the charging of fees for sacraments (opposing simony). The Second Lateran Council was an important milestone of the Gregorian Reform, strengthening papal control over the Church.

v.t: Victory at Yancheng and the twelve gold plaques
v.s: In June, Jin commander Wanyan Zongbi tore up the peace agreement and invaded south with 100,000 troops. Yue Fei led the Yue Family Army to meet the main Jin force at Yancheng (modern Luohe, Henan). Zongbi deployed his elite 'Iron Pagoda' heavy cavalry and 'Guai Zima' flanking light cavalry. Yue Fei ordered his infantry to use long swords and axes to cut the legs of horses while his cavalry charged. Yang Zaixing rode alone into the Jin lines, killing hundreds. The Jin army was routed; Zongbi wept: 'Since rising from the sea, I have always relied on these tactics to win; now they are finished!' Yue pursued to Zhuxianzhen, only 45 li from Kaifeng. But Emperor Gaozong, fearing Yue's growing power and egged on by Qin Hui, issued twelve gold plaques in a single day ordering Yue to withdraw. Yue lamented: 'Ten years of effort, ruined in one day!'

v.t: Completion of the Decretum Gratiani
v.s: The Benedictine monk Gratian in Bologna compiled the Concordia discordantium canonum (usually known as the Decretum Gratiani). The work collected all canonical decrees from the ancient period to the 12th century, including council decisions, patristic writings, and papal decretals, reconciling contradictions. The Decretum is divided into three parts: Part I discusses sources of canon law; Part II deals with ecclesiastical affairs (orders, sacraments, church property); Part III concerns the celebration of sacraments. The Decretum became the standard textbook of canon law in the Middle Ages, forming the basis of the Catholic Church's code until it was replaced in 1917.

v.t: Treaty of Shaoxing signed
v.s: In November, the Song and Jin signed the Treaty of Shaoxing. Its main terms: 1) The Southern Song became a vassal state of the Jin, accepting Jin investiture; 2) The border was fixed along the Huai River to the Dasanguan Pass (southwest of modern Baoji, Shaanxi); 3) The Southern Song paid an annual tribute of 250,000 taels of silver and 250,000 bolts of silk; 4) The Jin returned the remains of Emperor Huizong of Song and released Emperor Gaozong's mother, Empress Dowager Wei. The Treaty of Shaoxing was a humiliating peace for the Southern Song, but it bought nearly twenty years of peace, allowing the Jiangnan economy to recover. After the treaty, Gaozong and Qin Hui moved to strip the military commanders of their power.

v.t: Yue Fei, Zhang Xian, and Yue Yun executed
v.s: In October, Yue Fei was stripped of his military command and imprisoned on the trumped-up charge of 'maybe there is' (moxuyou). Han Shizhong confronted Qin Hui: 'How can the words “maybe there is” convince the people?' But Qin Hui insisted on the unjust execution. On December 29, Yue Fei was poisoned at Fengbo Pavilion, and his son Yue Yun and subordinate Zhang Xian were beheaded. Before his death, Yue Fei wrote the words 'The sun is bright, the sun is bright'. Yue Fei's unjust death provoked outrage among the Southern Song military and populace; many generals lost heart, and the cause of resistance to the Jin suffered a heavy blow. Twenty years later (1162), Emperor Xiaozong of Song posthumously rehabilitated Yue Fei, restored his official titles, and reburied him with full honors.

v.t: Remains of Emperor Huizong of Song returned south; Empress Dowager Wei returns to Song
v.s: In accordance with the Treaty of Shaoxing, the Jin returned the remains of Emperor Huizong of Song (allegedly in a wooden coffin) and his mother, Empress Dowager Wei, to the Southern Song. The Empress Dowager had been held captive for fifteen years in the north, forced to work in the Jin laundry service. After her return, Gaozong's queen, nee Xing, also returned, but Xing had already died, unbeknownst to Gaozong. The return of his mother relieved Gaozong's mind and reinforced his commitment to a policy of appeasement. Huizong's remains were interred at Yongyou Mausoleum in Shaoxing.

v.t: Completion of the Dà Sòng Chóngxiū Guǎngyùn
v.s: The Dà Sòng Chóngxiū Guǎngyùn (commonly known as the Guǎngyùn), commissioned by Emperor Zhenzong of Song, was compiled by Chen Pengnian, Qiu Yong, and others, and first published in 1142. The Guǎngyùn is the most complete and oldest extant rime dictionary in China. It has five volumes, 206 rime categories, and contains 26,194 characters. Based on Lu Fayan's Qièyùn from the Sui dynasty, it meticulously records the phonology of Middle Chinese (initials, finals, and tones) and is the most important document for the study of Chinese historical phonology. The Guǎngyùn profoundly influenced later rime dictionaries such as the Píngshuǐyùn.

v.t: Death of Byzantine Emperor John II; Manuel I ascends
v.s: On April 8, Byzantine Emperor John II Komnenos died of an arrow infection sustained during a hunt. John II had recovered much of western Asia Minor and forced the Principality of Antioch to submit. He was succeeded by his son Manuel I. Manuel was an ambitious emperor who sought to restore the empire to its Justinian-era borders. He invaded southern Italy (1155), competed with Holy Roman Emperor Frederick I for influence in Hungary, and allied with the Pope. However, Manuel's expansionism exhausted Byzantine resources, laying the groundwork for later decline.

v.t: World map by Arab geographer al-Idrisi
v.s: King Roger II of Sicily commissioned the Arab geographer Muhammad al-Idrisi to create a world map. Al-Idrisi spent fifteen years synthesizing Arab merchant accounts and classical sources, completing in 1143 the Book of Roger (also known as the Book of Pleasant Journeys). The book included a silver world map (now lost), divided the Earth into seven climatic zones, and depicted major cities, mountains, and rivers of Europe, Asia, and Africa. It was one of the most accurate world maps of the Middle Ages, reflecting the highest achievements of 12th-century Arab geography.

v.t: Fall of the County of Edessa, sparking the Second Crusade
v.s: On December 24, Imad al-Din Zengi, governor of Mosul, captured the city of Edessa (modern Şanlıurfa, Turkey), massacring all Christians there, and the County of Edessa came to an end. Edessa was the first Crusader state (founded 1098), and its fall shocked Christendom. Pope Eugenius III issued the bull Quantum Praedecessores in 1145, calling for the Second Crusade. King Louis VII of France and Holy Roman Emperor Conrad III responded, leading armies that set out in 1147. The Second Crusade ended in failure, but it did enhance East-West contacts.

v.t: Compilation of the Pingshuiyun rime system
v.s: During the Southern Song period, Liu Yuan of Pingshui (modern Linfen, Shanxi) under Jin rule compiled the Renzixin Kanli Bù Yùnlüè, merging the 206 rime categories of the Guǎngyùn into 107. This rime system became known as the Pingshuiyun and served as the official rhyme book for the imperial examinations in the Yuan, Ming, and Qing dynasties. The Pingshuiyun was based on the northern Mandarin of the time, reflecting the transition from Middle Chinese to Early Mandarin. It is still used by poets writing in the old style today.

v.t: Pope Eugenius III calls for the Second Crusade
v.s: On February 15, Pope Eugenius III issued the bull Quantum Praedecessores from Rome, calling on Western European rulers and knights to undertake a second crusade to recover Edessa. The bull emphasized that crusaders would receive an indulgence (remission of sins) and protection of their property and families. King Louis VII of France and Holy Roman Emperor Conrad III were the first to respond. Bernard of Clairvaux preached at Vézelay in France, where tens of thousands took the cross. The Second Crusade was larger in scale than the First.

v.t: Period of relative peace along the Song-Jin border
v.s: After the Treaty of Shaoxing, the Song-Jin border enjoyed over a decade of peace. The Southern Song government built several border markets (ques) along the Huai River for trade with the Jin. The Song exported tea, silk, porcelain, and books; the Jin exported horses, furs, and ginseng. The Song's annual tax revenue from the border markets exceeded one million strings. The peace allowed the Southern Song to focus on developing the Jiangnan economy, introducing early-ripening drought-resistant Champa rice, making Jiangnan the 'granary of the empire'. However, the Song rulers became content with their southern refuge and abandoned any thought of northern campaigns.

v.t: Bernard of Clairvaux preaches the Second Crusade
v.s: On March 31 (Easter Sunday), Bernard of Clairvaux preached on a wheat field at Vézelay in France, calling the French people to join the Second Crusade. According to records, King Louis VII and many nobles knelt and took the cross from Bernard's hands. Bernard then traveled to Germany and preached to Conrad III at Speyer Cathedral; Conrad also took the cross. Bernard's sermons were highly emotional, describing the suffering of eastern Christians and promising the remission of sins. The launching of the Second Crusade was largely due to Bernard's personal influence.

v.t: Early beginnings of Oxford University
v.s: In 1146, a group of scholars and students left the University of Paris and settled in Oxford, forming the nucleus of Oxford University. At the time, the University of Paris was in turmoil due to conflicts between Abelard and conservative churchmen, and some English scholars chose to return home to teach. Oxford initially focused on theology and the liberal arts (the seven arts), later developing law and medicine. In 1167, King Henry II of England banned English students from studying at Paris, and Oxford grew rapidly. Oxford adopted a tutorial system (one-on-one or small-group teaching), becoming a model for British higher education.

v.t: Second Crusade begins
v.s: In May, Conrad III led the German crusader army (about 20,000 men) from Regensburg east along the Danube. In June, Louis VII led the French crusader army (about 20,000) from Paris. The armies met at Constantinople, where Byzantine Emperor Manuel I, suspicious of the crusaders, urged them to cross the Bosporus quickly. After entering Asia Minor, Conrad's army was routed by the Seljuk Turks near Dorylaeum in October; Conrad was wounded and fled back to Constantinople. Louis VII's army was ambushed in January of the following year and suffered heavy losses. The remnants of the crusade reached Antioch in March 1148. The Second Crusade ended in failure.

v.t: Moscow first mentioned in chronicles
v.s: In 1147, Moscow was first mentioned in Russian chronicles. That year, Yuri Dolgoruky, Grand Prince of Suzdal, invited his ally, Prince Svyatoslav Olgovich of Novgorod-Seversky, to a feast on the border at Moscow. At that time, Moscow was only a small village on the border of Kievan Rus', but Yuri built a wooden fortress (predecessor of the Kremlin) there. The year 1147 is traditionally considered the founding year of Moscow. Centuries later, Moscow grew into the capital and largest city of Russia.

v.t: Founding of Portugal and reconquest of Lisbon
v.s: In June, Count Afonso Henriques of Portugal, with the help of English and French crusaders, captured Lisbon from the Moors. Afonso had proclaimed himself king (Afonso I) in 1139, but it was only after the reconquest of Lisbon in 1147 that his kingship was recognized by the Pope (1179). Portugal became one of the oldest nation-states in Europe, separating from the Kingdom of León. Afonso built St. George's Castle and the Jerónimos Monastery in Lisbon and moved the capital from Coimbra to Lisbon. Portuguese independence inaugurated nearly a thousand years of its history.

v.t: Second Crusade fails in siege of Damascus
v.s: In July, the remnants of the Second Crusade (about 50,000 men), under the joint command of King Baldwin III of Jerusalem, Conrad III, and Louis VII, laid siege to Damascus. The crusaders chose to attack Damascus because they mistakenly believed it to be the weakest point of the Muslims. However, Damascus's walls were strong and the defenders fought tenaciously. The siege lasted only four days before the crusaders withdrew due to supply difficulties and internal discord. This failure marked the complete failure of the Second Crusade. Conrad III and Louis VII returned to Europe in 1149; the crusade had failed to recapture Edessa or gain any new territory.

v.t: Henry the Lion becomes Duke of Saxony
v.s: Henry of the Welf dynasty (nicknamed 'the Lion') succeeded his father as Duke of Saxony. Henry was one of the most powerful princes of the Holy Roman Empire, dedicated to expanding eastward across the Elbe into Slavic lands. He conquered Mecklenburg and Pomerania, forcing local Slavic chiefs to convert to Christianity. Henry also built cities such as Munich, Lübeck, and Bremen, promoting trade on the North and Baltic Seas. He was a key supporter of Emperor Frederick I (Barbarossa), but later fell out with the emperor and was stripped of his lands.

v.t: Wanyan Liang murders the emperor and usurps the throne
v.s: On January 9, Wanyan Liang, a member of the Jin imperial family (grandson of Jin Taizu), with the support of Wanyan Zongbi, staged a coup and killed Emperor Xizong of Jin. Wanyan Liang proclaimed himself emperor (Prince Hailing of Jin). Prince Hailing was an ambitious but cruel ruler; he moved the capital to Yanjing (modern Beijing) and began a large-scale southern campaign in an attempt to unify China. He brutally suppressed opposition among the imperial clan, killing more than seventy sons of Emperor Taizong and over 130 other clan members. Hailing also forced Jurchens to speak Chinese, adopt Chinese surnames, and wear Chinese clothing, accelerating the sinicization of the Jin. In 1161, while on a campaign against the Song, he was killed by his own officers.

v.t: Birth of Islamic philosopher Ibn Tufayl
v.s: The Arab philosopher Ibn Tufayl (c. 1105–1185) was one of the greatest thinkers of the 12th-century Islamic world. Born in Granada (modern southern Spain), he served as a vizier of the Almohad dynasty. His philosophical novel Hayy ibn Yaqdhan (The Living Son of the Awake) tells the story of a boy raised alone on a desert island who, through reason, explores nature and the soul, ultimately achieving knowledge of God. The book was translated into Latin and various European languages, influencing the European Enlightenment, and is considered the precursor of the philosophical novel.

v.t: Angkor Wat completed
v.s: Khmer King Suryavarman II died, but the construction of Angkor Wat, which he had commissioned, was essentially complete. Angkor Wat is the largest religious building in the world: 1.5 km from east to west, 1.3 km from north to south, with a central tower 65 m high. The entire structure is made of sandstone blocks fitted without mortar. The relief carvings at Angkor Wat total 800 meters in length, depicting Hindu myths and the military campaigns of the Khmer Empire. Suryavarman II was a devotee of Vishnu, claiming to be an incarnation of the god. Angkor Wat served not only as a temple but also as the king's mausoleum.
