It is the highest achievement of Khmer civilization and was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1992.

v.t: Rise of European universities — University of Paris receives charter
v.s: In 1150, the University of Paris (then known as the guild of teachers and students of Paris) was officially recognized by King Louis VII of France. The university initially comprised the faculty of theology, law, medicine, and arts (the seven liberal arts). It was the most influential university in medieval Europe, attracting students from England, Germany, Italy, and other countries. Famous scholastics such as Thomas Aquinas, Bonaventure, and Roger Bacon taught there. The organizational model of the University of Paris (governance by professors) became a model for other European universities.

v.t: Initial spread of Arabic numerals in Europe
v.s: French and Italian scholars, through the translation movement in Spain, began to become acquainted with Arabic numerals (0–9) and the decimal system. Arabic numerals were far more convenient than Roman numerals (I, V, X, L, C, D, M) for arithmetic, especially because they included the concept of zero. Italian merchants were the first to adopt Arabic numerals for bookkeeping; later mathematicians and scientists also adopted the new system. The spread of Arabic numerals was one of the most important milestones in the history of European mathematics.

v.t: Golden age of the Kingdom of Georgia begins
v.s: During the reign of King Demetrius I of Georgia (1125–1156), Georgian art, literature, and architecture flourished. He built the Church of St. Nino in Tbilisi and minted silver coins with Georgian inscriptions. Georgia's golden age reached its peak under Queen Tamar (1184–1213), when Georgian influence extended to the southeastern coast of the Black Sea. During this period, Georgia built many churches and monasteries, such as the Vardzia cave monastery complex.

v.t: Prince Hailing of Jin moves capital to Yanjing
v.s: In April, Prince Hailing of Jin ordered the capital moved from Shangjing (Huining Prefecture, modern Acheng, Harbin) to Yanjing (modern Beijing), renamed Zhongdu. Hailing considered Shangjing too remote to effectively rule the Central Plains. He expanded the city of Yanjing on the basis of the Liao's Nanjing, building a wall about 30 li in circumference with thirteen gates. Zhongdu was modeled on the layout of the Northern Song capital Kaifeng, with an inner palace, an imperial city, and an outer city. Hailing forced Jurchen nobles and Meng'an-Mouke households to move south and razed the palaces and ancestral temples of Shangjing to prevent their return. The move strengthened Jin control over the Central Plains but also accelerated the sinicization of the Jurchens.

v.t: European scholasticism — Peter Lombard's Four Books of Sentences
v.s: Peter Lombard (c. 1100–1160), bishop of Paris, compiled the Four Books of Sentences (Libri Quattuor Sententiarum). The work systematically organized the sayings of the Church Fathers and ancient writers into three parts: the Trinity, the Incarnation, sacraments, and eschatology. Lombard used the 'question method', presenting various views on each question before giving his own conclusion. The Sentences became the standard theological textbook in medieval universities and remained in use until the 16th century. Thomas Aquinas's Summa Theologica was also influenced by it. Peter Lombard became bishop of Paris in 1159.

v.t: Frederick I becomes King of Germany
v.s: On March 4, Conrad III died, and his nephew Frederick I (nicknamed 'Barbarossa', meaning 'Red Beard') was elected King of Germany. Frederick I belonged to the Hohenstaufen family, and his mother was a Welf, so he tried to reconcile the two feuding houses. He was crowned Holy Roman Emperor in Rome in 1155. Barbarossa spent his life trying to restore imperial power in Italy, engaging in long struggles with Pope Alexander III and the Lombard League. He was one of the most famous medieval German emperors, and his image became a symbol of the German nation.

v.t: Louis VII divorces Eleanor of Aquitaine
v.s: On March 21, King Louis VII of France divorced his queen, Eleanor of Aquitaine, on grounds of consanguinity and failure to bear a male heir. Eleanor took the Duchy of Aquitaine, her dowry, with her, significantly reducing the French royal domain. In May of the same year, Eleanor married Henry of England (the future Henry II), bringing Aquitaine into the English crown as her dowry. This gave Henry II possession of more French territory (Normandy, Anjou, Aquitaine) than Louis VII himself. The resulting conflict between England and France was a long-term cause of the Hundred Years' War.

v.t: Completion of the Jin central capital (Zhongdu)
v.s: After moving the capital from Shangjing to Yanjing, Prince Hailing of Jin ordered the expansion of Yanjing into the new capital, Zhongdu. In 1153, construction of Zhongdu was essentially complete. The city was modeled on the layout of the Northern Song capital Kaifeng, with walls about 30 li in circumference and thirteen gates. The palace city was located on the central axis at the main south gate, containing the imperial ancestral temple, the altars of soil and grain, and the palace complex. The construction of Zhongdu marked the beginning of Beijing's role as a national capital. The ruins of Jin Zhongdu lie in the area of modern Guang'anmen, Beijing.

v.t: Treaty of Constance signed
v.s: Frederick I and Pope Eugenius III signed the Treaty of Constance. According to the treaty, Frederick recognized the Pope's temporal rule over the city of Rome and its surrounding territory and promised not to interfere in papal elections at his coronation. The Pope recognized Frederick as Holy Roman Emperor. The treaty temporarily eased the conflict between emperor and pope, but later disputes over Italy would rekindle hostilities.

v.t: Henry II inherits the English throne, founding the Plantagenet dynasty
v.s: On October 25, King Stephen of England died, and Henry II of Anjou succeeded according to the Treaty of Wallingford, founding the Plantagenet (Angevin) dynasty. Henry II ruled a vast territory stretching from the Scottish border to the Pyrenees (England, Normandy, Anjou, Aquitaine), known as the 'Angevin Empire'. He carried out judicial reforms, establishing circuit courts and the jury system, laying the foundation of the common law system. He also came into conflict with Thomas Becket, Archbishop of Canterbury, leading to Becket's murder. Henry II was one of the most influential monarchs of 12th-century Europe.

v.t: Prince Hailing of Jin mints Zhenglong Tongbao coins
v.s: Prince Hailing of Jin minted 'Zhenglong Tongbao' copper coins, the first Jin coins to bear a reign title. The coins were modeled on the circular square-holed coins of the Song, with the inscription in regular script Chinese characters. Before this, the Jin had mainly used old Liao and Song coins and paper money; minting their own currency marked the economic independence and development of the Jin dynasty. The minting of Zhenglong Tongbao promoted the commercial economy but also exacerbated inflation.

v.t: Malcolm IV becomes King of Scotland
v.s: On May 24, King David I of Scotland died, and his grandson Malcolm IV (the Maiden, 1141–1165) succeeded. Malcolm was only thirteen years old and came under strong pressure from King Henry II of England. In 1157, Malcolm was forced to return Cumberland and Westmorland to England and recognize Henry's suzerainty over Northumbria. Malcolm was a devout Christian who patronized the building of several monasteries.

v.t: Frederick I crowned Holy Roman Emperor
v.s: On June 18, Frederick I was crowned Holy Roman Emperor by Pope Adrian IV at St. Peter's Basilica in Rome. At the ceremony, a dispute arose: Frederick refused to hold the Pope's stirrup and lead his horse, so the Pope refused to crown him. After several hours of stalemate, Frederick reluctantly submitted. After the coronation, Frederick ordered the execution of Arnold of Brescia, a leader of the Roman commune who had preached the abolition of the Pope's secular power. Frederick later invaded Italy multiple times, seeking to reassert imperial control over the Italian cities.

v.t: Establishment of the Commune of Florence
v.s: The nobles and merchants of the central Italian city of Florence formed a 'commune' (self-governing body). The Florentine commune consisted of a signoria (elected magistrates) and a popular assembly (composed of all citizens). The formation of the commune marked Florence's liberation from episcopal rule and control by feudal nobles. Florence later developed into Europe's most important financial center and the birthplace of the Renaissance. Florentine banking families (such as the Medici) controlled the finances of the Pope and European monarchs.

v.t: Heiji Rebellion in Japan
v.s: In 1156, Japan saw the Hōgen Rebellion, followed by the Heiji Rebellion (1159–1160). The Hōgen Rebellion was a civil war between Emperor Sutoku and Emperor Go-Shirakawa over the succession, with Minamoto no Yoshitomo and Taira no Kiyomori supporting different sides. In the Heiji Rebellion, Minamoto no Yoshitomo attempted to seize power but was defeated by Taira no Kiyomori; Yoshitomo was killed. The remaining Minamoto forces (including the 13-year-old Minamoto no Yoritomo) were exiled. Taira no Kiyomori thereby gained control of the imperial court, establishing the Taira regime. The Heiji Rebellion was a critical turning point when the samurai class replaced the court nobility in national power.

v.t: Yun In-son assassinates the king; beginning of military rule in Goryeo
v.s: King Uijong of Goryeo (r. 1146–1170) was known for his debauchery and favoritism toward corrupt officials. In 1170, military officers Jeong Jung-bu and Yi Ui-bang staged a coup, deposing Uijong, killing many civil officials, and installing King Myeongjong. Thereafter, Goryeo entered an era of military rule (1170–1270), with military officers wielding power. In 1156, the Goryeo ruler began a 'northern expansion policy', attacking Jurchen tribes east of the Yalu River in an attempt to recover former Goguryeo territory, but met with opposition from the Jin dynasty.

v.t: Byzantine–Hungarian War
v.s: Byzantine Emperor Manuel I invaded the Kingdom of Hungary, capturing Sirmium and Belgrade. Manuel's goal was to control the Danube waterway and prevent Hungary from allying with the Kingdom of Sicily. King Géza II of Hungary sued for peace, ceding Dalmatia and parts of Croatia. Byzantine expansion reached its peak in the 1160s. Manuel also attempted to conquer the Norman lands of southern Italy but failed.

v.t: Almohad dynasty establishes capital at Marrakesh
v.s: The Almohad dynasty of North Africa (1121–1269), under Abd al-Mu'min, conquered Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, and southern Spain. In 1157, Abd al-Mu'min made Marrakesh his capital, building the Kutubiyya Mosque (77 m high) and palaces. The Almohads adhered to strict monotheism and opposed the tolerant policies of the Almoravids. In Spain, they persecuted Jews and Christians, provoking counterattacks by Christian kingdoms. The Almohads were defeated by a Christian coalition at the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212, after which they gradually declined.

v.t: Frederick I attacks Milan
v.s: Frederick I besieged the northern Italian city of Milan, the wealthiest city in Lombardy and a center of resistance to imperial authority. Frederick defeated the Milanese army at Casano near Brescia, then besieged Milan. In September 1158, Milan surrendered, and Frederick was crowned King of Italy in Milan Cathedral. He appointed his nephew as governor of Milan and forced the city to pay a huge indemnity. Milan rebelled again in 1162, and Frederick besieged and completely destroyed the city (preserving only the churches). Frederick's tyranny led to the formation of the Lombard League (1167) by the Lombard cities.

v.t: Oxford University receives royal charter
v.s: In 1158, King Henry II of England issued a decree forbidding secular courts from hearing disputes between Oxford scholars and students, or between them and local residents, instead assigning such cases to the bishop's court. This effectively gave the university judicial autonomy. Oxford thereby became a true university. Oxford specialized in theology and philosophy, producing famous scholars such as Roger Bacon and John Wycliffe. Oxford's collegiate system (e.g., Merton College, 1264) later became a model for American and British universities.

v.t: Election of Pope Alexander III and papal schism
v.s: On September 7, Pope Adrian IV died. The cardinals elected Rolando Bandinelli as Pope Alexander III. However, a minority of cardinals loyal to Holy Roman Emperor Frederick I simultaneously elected Victor IV as antipope. Frederick I convened the Council of Pavia (1160), recognizing Victor IV as the legitimate pope. Alexander III fled to France, seeking the protection of King Louis VII. The schism lasted 17 years (until 1177), one of the greatest schisms in medieval church history. Alexander III was eventually recognized by England, France, Spain, and Hungary, and Frederick was forced to recognize Alexander in the Treaty of Venice (1177).

v.t: Southern Song issues paper currency 'Huizi'
v.s: In 1159, the Southern Song government began issuing paper money called 'Huizi'—the first government-issued paper currency in the world. Initially only circulated in Lin'an, it was later extended nationwide. Denominations were one, two, and three strings, exchangeable for copper coins. The Song issued new Huizi every three years (a 'series'), with each series having a total face value of 10 million strings. The issuance of Huizi promoted commercial development but also caused inflation (e.g., in the 1240s, Huizi had depreciated to one-tenth of copper coin value). The invention of paper money was a major revolution in financial history.

v.t: Emperor Xiaozong ascends the throne of Song, determined to recover the north
v.s: In 1162, Emperor Gaozong abdicated in favor of his adopted son Zhao Shen, who became Emperor Xiaozong of Song, the only Southern Song emperor determined to recover the Central Plains. He posthumously rehabilitated Yue Fei, restoring his official titles and reburying him with honors. He also recalled the hawkish ministers who had been exiled by Qin Hui (such as Zhang Jun and Hu Quan), preparing for a northern expedition. In 1163, Xiaozong launched the Longxing Northern Expedition but was defeated by the Jin. He was forced to conclude the Treaty of Longxing, which kept peace for about 40 years.

v.t: Rise of European universities — foundation of Cambridge University
v.s: In 1160, some scholars and students from Oxford, after conflicts with local residents, moved to Cambridge and established Cambridge University. Cambridge initially followed Oxford's teaching model, with faculties of theology, mathematics, law, and medicine. In 1284, Cambridge founded its first college, Peterhouse. Cambridge has been particularly prominent in the natural sciences; Newton, Darwin, and Maxwell studied or worked there.

v.t: Battle of Caishi — Yu Yunwen defeats Wanyan Liang
v.s: In November, Prince Hailing of Jin, Wanyan Liang, led 600,000 troops south, advancing to the Yangtze River at Caishi (near modern Ma'anshan, Anhui). The Song commander, Liu Qi, was seriously ill; Emperor Gaozong ordered the civilian official Yu Yunwen to go to Caishi to reward the troops. When Yu arrived, he found only 18,000 demoralized Song soldiers. He resolutely organized a defense, ordering the Song ships to ram Jin boats with fast paddle-wheel ships (sea hawk ships). The Jin, unaccustomed to naval warfare, suffered heavy casualties. Wanyan Liang was forced to move to Yangzhou, planning to cross at Guazhou, but a mutiny broke out among his officers, and he was killed. The Battle of Caishi was a major victory for the Southern Song, preventing its collapse.

v.t: Coup at the Jin Eastern Capital (Liaoyang)
v.s: While Wanyan Liang was campaigning south, Wanyan Yong, the defender of the Jin Eastern Capital (Liaoyang Prefecture, modern Liaoyang, Liaoning), and a grandson of Jin Taizu, staged a coup and proclaimed himself emperor, becoming Emperor Shizong of Jin. Shizong immediately sued for peace with the Southern Song and withdrew his forces from the south. After his defeat at Caishi, Wanyan Liang was killed by his own mutinous troops. Emperor Shizong became known as the 'Little Yao and Shun' of the Jin; he reformed abuses, reduced taxes, maintained peace with the Song, and ushered in a golden age (the Dading reign, 1161–1189).

v.t: Russian literature — The Tale of Igor's Campaign
v.s: In 1185, Prince Igor Svyatoslavich of Novgorod-Seversk led an expedition against the Polovtsians (a Turkic tribe) and was captured after defeat. This event was later turned into the epic poem The Tale of Igor's Campaign, the greatest work of Old Russian literature. Around 1161, the political fragmentation of Kievan Rus' was intensifying, with frequent wars among the principalities, laying the ground for later Mongol invasion.

v.t: Emperor Gaozong abdicates; Emperor Xiaozong ascends
v.s: In June, Emperor Gaozong of Song abdicated, citing exhaustion, in favor of his adopted son Zhao Shen (Emperor Xiaozong). Gaozong retired to the De'an Palace, where he lived as retired emperor for another 25 years (until 1187). Emperor Xiaozong was a seventh-generation descendant of Zhao Kuangyin (Gaozong, having no sons, selected an heir from the descendants of Taizu). After ascending, Xiaozong immediately rehabilitated Yue Fei, posthumously awarding him the title of Grand Preceptor and the posthumous name Wumu. He also restored the official titles of Yue's subordinates Zhang Xian and Yue Yun. Determined to recover the Central Plains, Xiaozong appointed hawkish ministers such as Zhang Jun, preparing for the Longxing Northern Expedition.

v.t: Thomas Becket becomes Archbishop of Canterbury
v.s: On June 3, King Henry II of England appointed his close friend and former Lord Chancellor Thomas Becket as Archbishop of Canterbury, hoping Becket would help him control the Church. However, after becoming archbishop, Becket changed his stance, vigorously defending the independence of the Church and opposing royal interference in ecclesiastical affairs. In 1164, Henry II issued the Constitutions of Clarendon, stipulating that clergy who committed crimes should be tried in secular courts. Becket refused to sign and fled to France. He returned to England in 1170 and was murdered in Canterbury Cathedral on December 29 by four of Henry's knights. Becket's assassination shocked Europe, and he was canonized as a saint.

v.t: Longxing Northern Expedition — Rout at Fuli
v.s: In April, Emperor Xiaozong appointed Zhang Jun as Commissioner of Military Affairs to lead the northern campaign. Zhang Jun sent two armies under Li Xianzhong and Shao Hongyuan north from the Huai River, initially making good progress, capturing Lingbi, Hongxian, and Suzhou (modern Suzhou, Anhui). But Li and Shao were at odds, and at Fuli (north of Suzhou) they were defeated by Jin general Heshi Lie Zhi Ning. The Song army suffered heavy losses, losing all its supplies. The Fuli rout ended Xiaozong's northern expedition, and the Jin counterattacked to the Huai River line. Xiaozong was forced to sue for peace.

v.t: Construction of Notre-Dame de Paris begins
v.s: In 1163, Bishop Maurice de Sully of Paris laid the foundation stone for Notre-Dame de Paris on the Île de la Cité in the Seine. Notre-Dame is a masterpiece of Gothic architecture, using such innovations as pointed arches, rib vaulting, flying buttresses, and rose windows. The cathedral plan is a Latin cross, 130 m long, 48 m wide, and 35 m high. The west front has two towers 69 m tall. Construction took about 170 years (completed 1330). In April 2019, a fire severely damaged the cathedral, destroying the spire but preserving the main structure.

v.t: Treaty of Longxing signed
v.s: In December, the Song and Jin signed the Treaty of Longxing. Its main terms: 1) The Southern Song would no longer be a vassal state but rather a 'nephew-uncle' relationship (Jin Shizong as uncle, Song Xiaozong as nephew); 2) The Song's annual tribute was reduced to 200,000 taels of silver and 200,000 bolts of silk (a reduction of 50,000 each from the Treaty of Shaoxing); 3) The border was fixed again along the Huai River to the Dasanguan Pass as in the Treaty of Shaoxing. The Treaty of Longxing was one of the few minor diplomatic gains for the Southern Song (improving its nominal status). After the treaty, Song-Jin peace lasted about 40 years.

v.t: Constitutions of Clarendon issued
v.s: In January, King Henry II of England issued the Constitutions of Clarendon, attempting to limit the Church's judicial privileges. The constitutions stipulated that clergy who committed crimes should be tried in secular courts; appeal to the Pope was forbidden without royal permission; episcopal elections had to be held in the king's presence. Archbishop Thomas Becket refused to sign and fled to France. In 1166, Henry II issued the Assize of Clarendon, advancing judicial reform and establishing the circuit court and jury system.

v.t: Barbarossa's Imperial Diet
v.s: Frederick I held an Imperial Diet at Würzburg, declaring that the Roman Church was under imperial protection and recognizing Alexander III as the legitimate pope. Frederick's turnabout came because King William I of Sicily had allied with the Pope, threatening imperial interests in Italy. Frederick then invaded Italy to fight the Lombard League.

v.t: Andrei Bogolyubsky of Suzdal besieges Kiev
v.s: Andrei Bogolyubsky (Andrei I) was the son of Yuri Dolgoruky. In 1165, Andrei led his forces to besiege Kiev, sacked the city, and proclaimed himself Grand Prince of Kiev. However, he did not remain in Kiev, leaving his brother to govern while he returned to Suzdal. Andrei made the city of Vladimir the capital of the Suzdal principality, building the Golden Gate and the Dormition Cathedral. His rule marked the shift of the political center of the Rus' from Kiev to the northeast.

v.t: Judicial reforms of Henry II of England — Assize of Clarendon
v.s: King Henry II of England issued the Assize of Clarendon, further advancing judicial reform. Key measures included: 1) Establishment of circuit courts — royal judges periodically traveled to each county to hear cases; 2) Establishment of the jury system — a panel of 12 local residents was responsible for finding facts; 3) Uniformity of common law — the judgments of the king's court became the uniform legal standard for the country. These reforms laid the foundation of English common law and weakened the judicial powers of feudal lords and the church.

v.t: Rise of the Sakya school of Tibetan Buddhism
v.s: The founder of the Sakya school (the 'Grey Sect'), Könchok Gyalpo (1034–1102), built Sakya Monastery in 1073. By 1166, the fifth Sakya patriarch, Drakpa Gyaltsen (1147–1216), was in charge, beginning the development of Sakya esoteric and exoteric teachings. The Sakya school's core teaching is the Lamdre (Path and Result), and they permit monks to marry (while keeping certain precepts). In 1260, the Sakya leader Drogön Chögyal Phagpa was appointed Imperial Preceptor by Kublai Khan of the Yuan dynasty, and the Sakya school became the ruling theocratic power in Tibet.

v.t: Formation of the Lombard League
v.s: In April, sixteen cities of northern Italy, including Cremona, Mantua, Brescia, and Bologna, formed the 'Lombard League' at the monastery of Pontida. The league's purpose was to resist the tyranny of Holy Roman Emperor Frederick I and protect the autonomy of the cities. The league was supported by Pope Alexander III and the Kingdom of Sicily. The Lombard League built its own army and defeated Frederick I at the Battle of Legnano in 1176, forcing him to recognize the autonomy of the Italian cities.

v.t: Foundation of Oxford University
v.s: In 1167, a dispute between King Henry II of England and King Louis VII of France led Henry to ban English students from attending the University of Paris. Many English scholars and students returned from Paris to Oxford, stimulating the rapid development of Oxford University. That year, Oxford formally received a royal charter, becoming an independent educational institution. Oxford adopted a collegiate system, with the earliest colleges being Merton (1264) and Balliol (1266).

v.t: Early formation of Oxford University
v.s: In 1168, Oxford University already had over 2,000 students, divided into two 'nations': the Northerners (Scots and northern English) and the Southerners (southern English and Welsh). Each nation elected a 'proctor' to manage student affairs. Oxford scholars lived in halls (which later evolved into colleges) and organized their own teaching. Oxford's curriculum included theology, law, medicine, and arts (the seven liberal arts). The Oxford 'tutorial system' was established during this period.

v.t: Saladin becomes vizier in Egypt, founding the Ayyubid dynasty
v.s: In 1168, the Fatimid caliph al-Adid appointed Saladin (a Kurd, 1137–1193) as vizier. Saladin was the nephew of Shirkuh, a general of the Zengid dynasty. After Shirkuh's death in 1169, Saladin inherited his position. Saladin gradually consolidated his power in Egypt and, after the death of the Fatimid caliph in 1171, established the Ayyubid dynasty. Saladin is famous for his campaigns against the Crusaders, culminating in the reconquest of Jerusalem in 1187.

v.t: Norman invasion of Ireland
v.s: In May, Diarmait Mac Murchada, King of Leinster in Ireland, was exiled and fled to England seeking help from Henry II. Henry allowed Norman nobles (lords of the Welsh Marches), Richard de Clare (nicknamed 'Strongbow') and Roger de Clare, to lead an invasion of Ireland. In May 1169, the Norman army landed at Wexford, and in the following year captured Dublin. In 1171, Henry II himself landed with a large army, receiving the submission of the Irish kings. The Norman invasion began 800 years of English rule in Ireland.

v.t: Revival of Song academy education — Zhu Xi rebuilds White Deer Grotto Academy
v.s: In 1169, the Neo-Confucian philosopher Zhu Xi (1130–1200), while serving in Nankang Army (modern Jiujiang, Jiangxi), rebuilt the White Deer Grotto Academy. Zhu personally wrote the 'White Deer Grotto Academy Regulations', setting forth the 'Five Principles of Social Relations' (affection between father and son, righteousness between ruler and minister, distinction between husband and wife, order between elder and younger, faithfulness between friends) and the 'Order of Learning' (extensive learning, careful questioning, earnest thinking, clear discrimination, sincere practice). The White Deer Grotto Academy became a center for the spread of Neo-Confucianism, where Zhu lectured and trained many disciples (such as Lu Jiuyuan and Lü Zuqian).

v.t: Murder of Thomas Becket
v.s: On December 29, Thomas Becket, Archbishop of Canterbury, was murdered in Canterbury Cathedral by four knights (Richard le Breton, Reginald FitzUrse, Hugh de Morville, and William de Tracy). The knights were close associates of King Henry II of England, who had misinterpreted Henry's frustrated outburst ('Will no one rid me of this turbulent priest?') as a murder command. Becket's murder shocked Europe, and his tomb became a pilgrimage site. In 1173, Pope Alexander III canonized Becket as a saint. Henry II did public penance at Becket's tomb in 1174, walking barefoot for several miles and being flogged. Canterbury Cathedral thus became one of the most important pilgrimage centers of the Middle Ages.

v.t: Fan Chengda sent as envoy to the Jin
v.s: The Southern Song poet Fan Chengda (1126–1193) was sent as an envoy to the Jin, requesting the return of the land containing the tombs of the Northern Song emperors (in Gongyi, Henan) and seeking to modify the ceremony for receiving state documents. After arriving in Zhongdu, Fan spoke eloquently at court; although Emperor Shizong of Jin did not agree to return the tombs, he expressed admiration for Fan's integrity. On his journey, Fan wrote 72 poems recording the suffering of the people in the occupied territories and their longing for the old dynasty, later compiled as the Luolanlu. His works are valuable historical sources on northern society under Jin rule.

v.t: Flourishing of medieval Latin literature in Europe
v.s: During the 12th-century Renaissance, Latin literature saw new genres and styles. The 'School of Chartres', centered on the cathedral school there, championed classical Latin prose; its representative was John of Salisbury (c. 1115–1180), whose Metalogicon and Policraticus blended Latin classics with scholastic philosophy. At the same time, rhymed verse and lyric poetry proliferated, many examples found in the Carmina Burana. These Latin works broke from the dry religious didacticism of the early Middle Ages, showing humanist and naturalist tendencies.

v.t: Saladin establishes the Ayyubid dynasty
v.s: In September, the last Fatimid caliph, al-Adid, died. Saladin (1137–1193), as vizier, formally abolished the Fatimid dynasty and established the Ayyubid dynasty, recognizing the spiritual authority of the Abbasid caliph. He moved his capital from Cairo to Damascus and unified Egypt, Syria, Yemen, and the Hejaz (Mecca and Medina). Saladin adhered to Sunni Islam, suppressed Shiites and Christians, and built many mosques, madrasas, and hospitals. His rise directly threatened the Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem.

v.t: Emperor Shizong of Jin implements the 'Dading Golden Age'
v.s: During the reign of Emperor Shizong of Jin (1161–1189), a series of reforms were implemented: reduction of taxes (the one-third tax increase imposed during the Song-Jin war was remitted), relief for refugees, restoration of agricultural production; improvement of official administration, punishment of corruption; promotion of Jurchen language education, requiring Jurchen nobles to study Jurchen and horsemanship to prevent excessive sinicization; maintenance of peace with the Southern Song and flourishing border trade. In 1171, Shizong ordered the establishment of Jurchen prefectural schools for Jurchen children. The Jin reached its peak during this period, known as the 'Dading Golden Age' or 'Little Yao and Shun'.

v.t: Jin issues paper money (Jiaochao)
v.s: Emperor Shizong of Jin ordered the issuance of 'Jiaochao' as circulating paper currency. The Jiaochao was based on the value of copper coins, in denominations ranging from one to ten strings, and was declared to circulate alongside copper coins, with a new issue every seven years (a 'series'). The issuance of Jiaochao reflected the development of the Jin economy, but also laid the groundwork for later inflation (excessive issuance in the late Jin led to a collapse in value). The Jin inherited and developed the Song's paper currency system, influencing the monetary policies of the Yuan and Ming dynasties.

v.t: Coup of Jeong Jung-bu in Goryeo; military rule established
v.s: King Uijong of Goryeo (r. 1146–1170) was known for his debauchery and favoritism toward civilian officials, causing dissatisfaction among military officers. In 1170, military officers Jeong Jung-bu and Yi Ui-bang staged a coup, killing Uijong and many civil officials, and installing King Myeongjong. In 1172, Jeong further purged opponents, establishing a military regency. Thereafter, Goryeo entered the era of 'military rule' (1170–1270), with military officers (such as Jeong Jung-bu, Yi Ui-min, and Choe Chung-heon) wielding power and civil officials marginalized. Military rule lasted until the Mongol invasions.

v.t: Revolt of Henry II's sons
v.s: In April, three sons of King Henry II of England — Henry the Young King, Richard (the Lionheart), and Geoffrey — rebelled, encouraged by their mother Eleanor of Aquitaine and King Louis VII of France. They allied with William the Lion of Scotland, the Count of Flanders, and others, seeking to overthrow Henry II. The Young King even went to Paris to ally with Louis VII. The rebellion lasted eighteen months; Henry II, relying on loyal troops and his financial resources, eventually crushed the revolt, but Eleanor was imprisoned (until 1189). This civil war severely weakened the Angevin Empire.

v.t: Expansion of St. Mark's Basilica in Venice
v.s: St. Mark's Basilica in Venice (first built in 828) was rebuilt in 1063, and in 1173 a major expansion added the baptistery and the four bronze horses (plundered from Constantinople during the Fourth Crusade) at the entrance. The basilica follows the Byzantine Greek cross plan, and its interior is covered by over 4,000 square meters of golden mosaics depicting biblical scenes and the life of St. Mark. St. Mark's Basilica became the spiritual symbol and display of wealth of the Republic of Venice.

v.t: William the Lion, King of Scotland, captured
v.s: On July 13, William the Lion of Scotland was captured by English knights near Alnwick Castle. William had invaded northern England to support the rebellion of Henry II's sons. He was taken to Falaise Castle in Normandy and forced to sign the Treaty of Falaise: Scotland recognized English suzerainty, gave William's daughter as hostage, and placed the Scottish church under the jurisdiction of the Archbishop of Canterbury. Although Richard I annulled the treaty in 1189 to raise funds for a crusade, the unequal relationship between England and Scotland continued into the later Middle Ages.

v.t: Saladin conquers Syria
v.s: Taking advantage of the chaos following the death of Nur ad-Din, the Zengid ruler of Aleppo, Saladin entered Syria in 1174, capturing Homs, Hama, and other cities. In December, he entered Damascus, receiving a warm welcome from the populace. Saladin gradually absorbed Nur ad-Din's territories, unifying Egypt and Syria. His rise put great pressure on the Crusader states, and King Baldwin IV of Jerusalem was forced to conclude a temporary truce with Saladin.

v.t: Goose Lake Debate — philosophical dispute between Zhu Xi and Lu Jiuyuan
v.s: In May, the Neo-Confucian philosopher Zhu Xi (1130–1200) and the philosopher of the mind (xinxue) Lu Jiuyuan (1139–1193) held a scholarly debate at Goose Lake Temple in Xinyang Prefecture, Jiangxi (modern Yanshan County), known as the 'Goose Lake Debate'. Zhu Xi advocated 'investigating things to extend knowledge', arguing that principle (li) exists in all things and must be investigated through study and observation. Lu Jiuyuan argued that 'the mind is principle', that the principle of all things is within the mind and only requires 'realizing one's original mind'. Lü Zuqian served as moderator. The debate lasted three days without resolving their differences. The Goose Lake Debate is one of the most famous events in the history of Song-Ming Neo-Confucianism, marking the formation of different schools within it.

v.t: Coup in Goryeo — Yi Ui-bang killed
v.s: The military regime in Goryeo was plagued by internal strife. In 1175, another military officer, Gyeong Dae-seung, allied with the general Jeong Jung-bu to kill the powerful Yi Ui-bang. Gyeong then killed Jeong Jung-bu and monopolized power. The frequent coups within the military regime made Goryeo politics unstable, and the civilian officials were completely suppressed. These struggles paved the way for the establishment of the Choe military regime (1196).

v.t: Battle of Legnano — Lombard League defeats Frederick I
v.s: On May 29, Holy Roman Emperor Frederick I (Barbarossa) led about 15,000 knights into Italy, where near Milan at Legnano he was met by the army of the Lombard League (about 12,000 men, including infantry and cavalry). For the first time, the Lombard League used coordinated infantry and cavalry, with a phalanx of large shields and long spears to withstand the imperial knights' charge. Frederick fell from his horse and was thought dead; the German army fled. This battle was the first major defeat for German heavy cavalry. In 1177, Frederick was forced to sign the Treaty of Venice, recognizing Alexander III as legitimate pope and returning church lands. The Lombard League's victory solidified the autonomy of the Italian city-states.

v.t: Truce between the Ayyubids and the Kingdom of Jerusalem
v.s: After consolidating his rule in Syria, Saladin concluded a two-year truce with King Baldwin IV of Jerusalem ('the Leper King'). Although afflicted with leprosy, Baldwin was a capable military commander who had defeated Saladin at Montgisard in 1177. In 1176, both sides had internal problems (Saladin needed to strengthen his hold on Syria, Baldwin's leprosy was worsening) and chose to avoid war. The truce gave Saladin time to consolidate his forces.

v.t: Treaty of Venice — Frederick I's submission to the Pope
v.s: On July 24, Frederick I and Pope Alexander III signed the Treaty of Venice at St. Mark's Basilica. Frederick recognized Alexander III as the legitimate pope, publicly kissed the Pope's foot, and promised to restore church lands; the Pope revoked Frederick's excommunication and recognized his authority over imperial territories. The treaty also stipulated that the emperor could not interfere in papal elections and that investiture belonged to the Pope. It was another victory for the church over the emperor since the Investiture Controversy. Frederick thereafter turned his attention to southern Italy (the Kingdom of Sicily) and the Third Crusade.

v.t: Champa invasion of the Khmer Empire (Angkor)
v.s: King Jaya Indravarman IV of Champa invaded the Khmer Empire (Angkor), sacking the city of Angkor. The Khmer forces organized a naval counterattack on Lake Tonlé Sap and eventually drove out the Chams. However, the invasion exposed the vulnerability of Angkor's defenses. After Jayavarman VII ascended the throne in 1181, he defeated Champa and incorporated it into the Khmer Empire.

v.t: Song-Jin peace holds — relative quiet on the border
v.s: Since the Treaty of Longxing (1164), the Song-Jin border had enjoyed more than a decade of peace. The Southern Song government established border markets along the Huai River for trade: the Song exported tea, silk, porcelain, and books; the Jin exported horses, furs, and ginseng. In 1178, Emperor Xiaozong ordered the repair of Huai River dikes to guard against a possible surprise Jin attack. During this period, the Southern Song economy prospered and the population grew, but the military became slack, and Xiaozong's ambition to recover the north gradually faded.

v.t: Yelü Zhilugu ascends the throne of Western Liao
v.s: Yelü Zhilugu (r. 1178–1211) became the fifth ruler of the Western Liao (Qara Khitai). During his reign, the Western Liao declined, with vassal states (such as Khwarazm and Samarkand) breaking away. Yelü Zhilugu was a devout Buddhist who neglected state affairs, causing the Western Liao's hegemony in Central Asia to collapse. In 1211, the Naiman prince Kuchlug staged a coup, seizing the Western Liao. The decline of the Western Liao cleared the way for Genghis Khan's western campaigns.

v.t: Third Lateran Council
v.s: In March, Pope Alexander III convened the Third Lateran Council at the Lateran Palace in Rome, the 11th ecumenical council of the Catholic Church. The council confirmed the terms of the Treaty of Venice, declaring the reconciliation between Frederick I and the church effective. It also issued several decrees: requiring a two-thirds majority of cardinals for papal election (to prevent antipopes); forbidding simony; requiring bishops to conduct triennial visitations; and requiring churches to provide education to the poor. This council strengthened papal authority and established the papal election procedure (still in use today).

v.t: Minamoto no Yoritomo exiled to Izu
v.s: After Taira no Kiyomori defeated Minamoto no Yoshitomo in the Heiji Rebellion (1159–1160), the Minamoto clan was purged. Minamoto no Yoritomo (1147–1199), the third son of Yoshitomo, was spared execution because of his youth (13 years old) and exiled to Izu Province (modern Shizuoka Prefecture), confined to the island of Hirugako. He spent 20 years in exile, gaining the support of the local Hōjō clan. In 1179, Taira no Kiyomori staged a coup, imprisoning the retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa and taking control of the court. Minamoto no Yoritomo, from exile, secretly made contact with anti-Taira forces, preparing to raise an army.

v.t: Philip II becomes King of France
v.s: On September 18, King Louis VII of France died, and his son Philip II Augustus (1165–1223) succeeded at age 15. Philip II was one of the greatest kings of the Capetian dynasty. Through a series of military and diplomatic maneuvers, he gradually recovered French territories held by the English Plantagenets (Normandy, Anjou, Aquitaine). He supported Richard the Lionheart against his father Henry II, then allied with Henry's younger son John against Richard. Philip also built the Louvre fortress and granted a charter to the University of Paris. His reign laid the foundation for France's dominance in late medieval Europe.

v.t: Genpei War breaks out in Japan
v.s: In 1180, Prince Mochihito, son of Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa, issued a decree calling for the destruction of the Taira. Minamoto no Yoritomo raised an army in Izu, and other Minamoto branches followed. In October, Yoritomo defeated the Taira army under Taira no Koremori at the Battle of Fujigawa. Meanwhile, Minamoto no Yoshinaka (Kiso Yoshinaka) also raised forces in the Hokuriku region. The Genpei War (1180–1185) had begun. Taira no Kiyomori died in 1181, and under his son Taira no Munemori, the Taira forces retreated. In 1183, Yoshinaka entered Kyoto, and the Taira, with the child Emperor Antoku (Kiyomori's grandson), fled west.

v.t: Genpei War continues — death of Taira no Kiyomori
v.s: In March, the Taira regime leader Taira no Kiyomori (1118–1181) died of fever at age 64. After the Heiji Rebellion, Kiyomori had seized control of the court, promoted Song-Japan trade, and built Itsukushima Shrine. He was the first samurai to dominate the imperial court. After his death, his son Taira no Munemori succeeded him, but he lacked ability, and the Taira forces suffered successive defeats. That same year, Minamoto no Yoritomo established his base at Kamakura, setting up a Samurai-dokoro (military police) and a Monchūjo (judicial board), forming the early structure of a shogunate.

v.t: Jayavarman VII ascends the throne — zenith of the Angkorian Empire
v.s: In 1181, Jayavarman VII (c. 1120–1218) became king of the Khmer Empire. He was the greatest monarch of the Angkorian period, defeated the Cham invasion, and expanded Khmer territory into modern-day Laos, Thailand, and Myanmar. A follower of Mahayana Buddhism, he built Angkor Thom (centered on the Bayon temple) and Ta Prohm, among many other structures. Jayavarman VII's reign marked the last flowering of Khmer civilization.

v.t: Saladin rules Egypt and Syria
v.s: In 1182, Saladin completed the unification of Syria and Egypt. He ordered the construction of the Citadel of Cairo as a military fortress and administrative center. He also built a 12-km-long wall to defend against Crusader attacks. Saladin implemented Islamic law, established religious courts, and founded numerous madrasas (Islamic schools). He constructed highways between Damascus and Cairo, strengthening the link between the two halves of his domain. Saladin's rule laid the military and economic foundation for the reconquest of Jerusalem in 1187.

v.t: Death of Afonso I of Portugal
v.s: On December 6, Afonso I (r. 1112–1185; proclaimed himself king in 1139), the first king of Portugal, died. He had recaptured Lisbon in 1147 and received papal recognition in 1179. He was succeeded by his son Sancho I. With the support of the crown and church, Portugal continued to expand southward, finally conquering the Algarve in 1249, completing the Portuguese Reconquista.

v.t: Treaty of Constance — compromise between Lombard League and emperor
v.s: On June 25, Frederick I and the Lombard League signed the Treaty of Constance. Under the treaty, Frederick recognized the autonomy of the Lombard cities (Milan, Cremona, Brescia, etc.), allowing them to elect their own consuls, while the emperor retained nominal suzerainty. The cities were to pay a fixed annual tribute and provide supplies when the emperor passed through Italy. The Treaty of Constance marked a victory for the Lombard League, formalizing the city-state system of northern Italy.

v.t: Minamoto no Yoshinaka's rise and the battle for Kyoto
v.s: In May 1183, Minamoto no Yoshinaka (Kiso Yoshinaka) routed the Taira army at the Battle of Kurikara Pass and then entered Kyoto. The retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa bestowed an official title on him, but Yoshinaka lost popular support because his troops looted the capital. In November, Minamoto no Yoritomo sent his brothers Minamoto no Noriyori and Minamoto no Yoshitsune west to attack Yoshinaka. In January 1184, Yoshitsune defeated and killed Yoshinaka at the Battle of Ichinotani. Yoritomo solidified his leadership of the Minamoto clan.

v.t: Battle of Ichinotani and prelude to the naval Battle of Dan-no-ura in Japan
v.s: On February 7, 1184, Minamoto no Yoshitsune and Minamoto no Noriyori attacked the Taira stronghold at Ichinotani in Settsu Province (modern Kobe). Yoshitsune led his cavalry down a steep cliff, surprising the Taira from behind, and routed them, killing several Taira commanders. The Taira remnants fled to Shikoku and Kyushu. On March 24, 1185, Yoshitsune destroyed the Taira fleet at the Battle of Dan-no-ura. Taira no Munemori was captured, and the child Emperor Antoku was drowned by his grandmother. The Genpei War ended with Minamoto victory.

v.t: Alliance between the Holy Roman Empire and the Byzantine Empire
v.s: Frederick I sent his son Henry VI to negotiate a treaty with Byzantine Emperor Isaac II Angelos, agreeing to cooperate against the Kingdom of Sicily (Normans). The terms were: Byzantium would pay tribute to the empire, and Frederick would recognize Byzantine control over the Balkans. However, the treaty was not effectively implemented, because in 1185 the Normans invaded Byzantium and sacked Thessaloniki.

v.t: End of the Genpei War — Battle of Dan-no-ura
v.s: On March 24, Minamoto no Yoshitsune and Minamoto no Noriyori engaged the Taira fleet at Dan-no-ura in Nagato Province (modern Shimonoseki). The Taira fleet initially had the advantage, holding the upstream position. However, Yoshitsune took advantage of the tidal shift (the tide went out in the morning, grounding the Taira ships) and ordered his archers to concentrate on killing Taira sailors. Taira no Tomomori (Kiyomori's brother) drowned himself when defeat was certain. Taira no Munemori was captured, and the child Emperor Antoku was drowned by his grandmother. The Genpei War ended, and Minamoto no Yoritomo became the supreme military leader of Japan.

v.t: Establishment of the Second Bulgarian Empire
v.s: In October, the Bulgarian nobles Peter and Asen (later known as Tsar Peter IV and Tsar Ivan Asen I) led a revolt from Tarnovo against the Byzantine Empire, taking advantage of increased taxes under Emperor Isaac II Angelos (r. 1185–1195) and internal Byzantine strife. They successfully established an independent Bulgarian state, known as the 'Second Bulgarian Empire' (1185–1396), with its capital at Tarnovo (Veliko Tarnovo). The Asen brothers were recognized by Byzantium in the Treaty of Lovech (1187). Bulgaria's independence weakened Byzantine control over the Balkans.

v.t: Conflict between Philip II of France and Henry II of England intensifies
v.s: King Philip II of France exploited the conflicts among King Henry II of England's sons (Henry the Young King, Richard, Geoffrey, John) to sow discord. In 1186, after Henry the Young King and Geoffrey died, Henry II designated Richard as his heir. Philip then supported Richard, promising to restore French lands to him on condition that Richard marry Philip's sister Alice (originally betrothed to Henry II). Richard allied with Philip against his father. In 1189, Henry II was defeated and signed the Treaty of Azay-le-Rideau, dying shortly after.

v.t: Southern Song expands circulation of Huizi paper currency
v.s: The Southern Song government increased the total face value of each series of Huizi from 10 million strings to 20 million strings. Huizi circulated alongside copper coins, mainly in the regions of Lianghuai and Liangzhe. The spread of Huizi promoted commercial growth, but fiscal deficits caused Huizi to depreciate. In 1186, the government set the official exchange rate at 1 Huizi = 1 string of copper coins, but the actual market rate had dropped to 1.5 Huizi per string. The Song's paper currency system was one of the earliest state credit monetary systems in the world, with profound influence.

v.t: Battle of Hattin — Saladin recaptures Jerusalem
v.s: On July 4, the Ayyubid sultan Saladin, with about 30,000 troops, decisively defeated the army of the Kingdom of Jerusalem (about 20,000) at Hattin (near modern Tiberias, Israel). King Guy of Lusignan of Jerusalem, Raynald of Châtillon, the master of the Knights Templar, and others were captured. Saladin executed Raynald (for repeatedly violating truces) but treated the king mercifully. On October 2, Saladin entered Jerusalem. Unlike the crusaders in 1099, he did not slaughter the inhabitants but allowed Christians to leave peacefully after paying a ransom. The Kingdom of Jerusalem effectively ended, with only a few coastal cities like Tripoli and Antioch remaining. This event triggered the Third Crusade (1189–1192).

v.t: Death of Emperor Gaozong of Song
v.s: On November 9, Emperor Gaozong of Song (1107–1187) died at the De'an Palace in Lin'an at age 81. He had been retired emperor for 25 years and still had some political influence. Gaozong was the founding emperor of the Southern Song, reigning for 36 years (1127–1162). He is often criticized for executing Yue Fei and signing the Treaty of Shaoxing, but he also stabilized the Jiangnan region and established the foundations of the Southern Song. After his death, Emperor Xiaozong mourned for three years and abdicated in 1189 to his son Zhao Dun (Emperor Guangzong).

v.t: Revision and spread of Gratian's Decretum
v.s: The Decretum compiled by canon lawyer Gratian (c. 1090–1159) was widely circulated and revised in the late 12th century. In 1187, canon law scholars at the University of Paris produced a Commentary on the Decretum, incorporating new decretals of Pope Alexander III. The Decretum became the standard canon law textbook in medieval universities, establishing the basic code of the Catholic Church until it was replaced in 1917.

v.t: Preparation for the Third Crusade — Richard and Philip take the cross
v.s: In January 1188, King Henry II of England and King Philip II of France met in Normandy to discuss launching the Third Crusade. In November of that year, Henry II died, and his son Richard (the Lionheart) succeeded. In 1189, Richard and Philip officially took the cross at Vézelay in France, committing to the crusade. They raised funds by imposing the 'Saladin tithe' (one-tenth of income). Holy Roman Emperor Frederick I also set out with an army in 1189. The Third Crusade was a joint effort by the three most powerful monarchs of Europe.

v.t: Death of Emperor Shizong of Jin; accession of Emperor Zhangzong
v.s: On January 20, Emperor Shizong of Jin, Wanyan Yong, died at age 67. His grandson Wanyan Jing (Emperor Zhangzong) succeeded. Zhangzong (1168–1208) was the sixth emperor of the Jin dynasty, highly sinicized, skilled in poetry and calligraphy, and his temple name was Zhangzong. During his reign, the Jin continued sinicization, improved the examination system, and compiled the Dajin Jili, reaching a cultural peak. However, in his later years, corruption and military neglect allowed the Mongols to rise, preparing the stage for Jin's decline.

v.t: Emperor Xiaozong abdicates; Emperor Guangzong ascends the Song throne
v.s: On February 18, Emperor Xiaozong of Song abdicated in favor of his son Zhao Dun, who became Emperor Guangzong. Guangzong suffered from mental illness, was often paranoid, and was unable to resist his wife, Empress Li Fengniang. Empress Li dominated the court, sowing discord between Guangzong and his father, and Guangzong long refused to visit Xiaozong. In 1189, Guangzong named Li as empress, and she began interfering in state affairs, driving out chancellors. Song politics entered a period of Empress Li's interference (1189–1194), leading to confusion and inefficiency.

v.t: Third Crusade begins
v.s: In May 1189, Holy Roman Emperor Frederick I led about 100,000 men from Regensburg east along the Danube. On June 10, 1190, while crossing the Göksu River (southeastern Turkey), Frederick was thrown from his horse and drowned. Most of his army dispersed. In July of that year, King Philip II of France and King Richard I of England (the Lionheart) set out from Vézelay and Marseille respectively, arriving in the Holy Land in April 1191. The main battlefield of the Third Crusade was the city of Acre (near modern Haifa, Israel), where the fighting lasted over two years and eventually ended in a truce.

v.t: Richard I ascends the English throne and joins the crusade
v.s: On July 6, King Henry II of England died, and Richard I (the Lionheart, 1157–1199) succeeded. To raise funds for the crusade, Richard sold offices and lands, famously saying, 'I would sell London if I could find a buyer.' He departed from Marseille in July 1190, and on the way, while in Sicily, he clashed with the local king and sacked Messina. Richard also conquered Cyprus (1191), selling it to the Kingdom of Jerusalem to raise funds. Richard was courageous but lacked political finesse.

v.t: Drowning of Frederick I
v.s: On June 10, 1190, Holy Roman Emperor Frederick I (Barbarossa, c. 1122–1190) drowned while crossing the Göksu River (modern southern Turkey, near Silifke). As he rode his horse into the river, the current was swift and his armor was heavy; he was thrown and drowned. His death caused most of the German crusader army to disperse; only a small contingent continued east. Frederick's body was buried in Antioch (some accounts say it was preserved in vinegar and later interred in separate locations). His death greatly weakened the Third Crusade.

v.t: Muslims establish sultanate in Delhi — Qutb al-Din Aibak ascends
v.s: The Ghurid (Afghan) general Qutb al-Din Aibak captured Delhi around 1190 and established the first Islamic dynasty in India — the Mamluk dynasty (the first dynasty of the Delhi Sultanate, 1206–1290). He built the Qutb Minar (73 m high, a masterpiece of Islamic architecture) and the Quwwat-ul-Islam Mosque. Aibak had been a slave (hence the name of the dynasty) and was appointed governor of India by the Ghurid sultan. When the sultan died in 1206, Aibak proclaimed himself sultan. The establishment of the Delhi Sultanate marked the beginning of lasting Muslim rule in India.

v.t: Minamoto no Yoritomo establishes the Kamakura Shogunate
v.s: In 1190, Minamoto no Yoritomo was appointed Captain of the Right Imperial Guard by the retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa, and thereafter he gradually gained control of all military power in Japan. In 1192, he was formally appointed 'Sei-i Taishōgun' (Barbarian-subduing Great General), establishing a shogunate (military government) at Kamakura. The shogunate created the Samurai-dokoro (military police), Monchūjo (judicial board), and Kumonjo (administrative and financial board), forming an independent system of government. The imperial court was sidelined, and Japan entered nearly 700 years of shogunate rule (until the Meiji Restoration in 1868). The establishment of the Kamakura Shogunate was the greatest political turning point in Japanese history.

v.t: Richard the Lionheart captures Acre
v.s: On July 12, King Richard I of England and King Philip II of France, after a two-year siege, captured the port of Acre (north of modern Haifa, Israel), which was held by Saladin. The garrison surrendered, and about 2,700 Muslim soldiers were taken prisoner. Richard and Philip quarreled over command, and on July 31 Philip returned to France, leaving Richard to fight alone. Richard massacred the 2,700 Muslim prisoners (because Saladin failed to pay the ransom and exchange prisoners on time). Richard then marched south along the Mediterranean coast toward Jerusalem.

v.t: Emperor Guangzong of Song seriously ill; court chaotic
v.s: Emperor Guangzong's mental illness worsened. He often claimed illness to avoid holding court, and even refused to attend important ceremonies. Empress Li took advantage to interfere in state affairs, driving out the old ministers of Xiaozong's reign (such as Zhao Ruyu and Liu Zheng) and appointing corrupt officials (such as Han Tuozhou). In 1191, Guangzong, seriously ill, refused to take medicine and avoided seeing his father Xiaozong; when Xiaozong died, Guangzong did not preside over the funeral. Disappointment with Guangzong grew. In 1194, Zhao Ruyu and Han Tuozhou staged a coup, forcing Guangzong to abdicate in favor of his son Zhao Kuo (Emperor Ningzong).

v.t: Richard and Saladin sign a peace treaty
v.s: On September 2, King Richard I of England and Saladin signed a three-year truce at Jaffa (near modern Tel Aviv). The main terms: 1) The crusaders retained the coastal cities from Tyre to Jaffa (including Acre), but Jerusalem remained Muslim; 2) Saladin allowed Christian pilgrims free access to Jerusalem; 3) Exchange of prisoners. After the treaty, Richard sailed away from the Holy Land on October 9, but was captured en route by Duke Leopold V of Austria, who turned him over to Emperor Henry VI (Frederick I's son). Richard was imprisoned for nearly two years, until 1194 when a huge ransom was paid. The Third Crusade was over.

v.t: Minamoto no Yoritomo appointed Shogun; Kamakura Shogunate officially established
v.s: On July 12, Minamoto no Yoritomo was formally appointed 'Sei-i Taishōgun' by the retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa. He established the shogunate at Kamakura as a military governing body. The imperial court was sidelined, and real power passed to the samurai class. Yoritomo implemented the 'Gokenin' (housemen) system, establishing lord-vassal relationships with warriors, granting them land (on) in exchange for military service (hōkō). He also formulated the legal code Goseibai Shikimoku (1232). The Kamakura Shogunate's establishment marked the beginning of medieval Japanese feudalism.

v.t: Death of Saladin
v.s: On March 4, Saladin (1137–1193) died of illness in Damascus at age 56. At his death, the entire treasury of the Ayyubid dynasty contained only one dinar and 47 dirhams (his generosity and wars had exhausted his wealth). Saladin was buried in a mausoleum near the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus. After his death, the Ayyubid realm was divided among his 17 sons: Egypt to al-Aziz Uthman, Damascus to al-Afdal, Aleppo to al-Zahir. Fraternal infighting led to the rapid decline of the Ayyubids. Saladin is revered in the Islamic world for recapturing Jerusalem and treating his enemies with magnanimity.

v.t: Death of Lu Jiuyuan
v.s: On January 18, the Southern Song philosopher and founder of the School of Mind, Lu Jiuyuan (1139–1193), died of illness. He proposed that 'the universe is my mind, my mind is the universe' and 'the mind is principle', arguing that truth is understood by 'realizing one's original mind'. His Goose Lake Debate with Zhu Xi (1175) is a landmark event in the history of Song-Ming Neo-Confucianism. Lu's philosophy of mind directly influenced Wang Yangming of the Ming, and they are jointly referred to as 'Lu-Wang School of Mind'.

v.t: Zhao Ruyu enthrones Emperor Ningzong of Song
v.s: In June, after Emperor Xiaozong of Song died and Emperor Guangzong, due to his mental illness, failed to attend the funeral, the court was in an uproar. The Administrator of the Privy Council, Zhao Ruyu, and the imperial in-law Han Tuozhou (nephew of Empress Gaozong's empress) jointly staged a coup, forcing Guangzong to abdicate in favor of his son Zhao Kuo (Emperor Ningzong). Zhao Kuo ascended the throne in July as Emperor Ningzong. Guangzong was forced to retire as retired emperor (he died in 1200). After Ningzong's accession, Zhao Ruyu was appointed chancellor, and Han Tuozhou gained power. But Han and Zhao soon fell out; in 1195 Han Tuozhou ousted Zhao Ruyu and monopolized power.

v.t: Holy Roman Emperor Henry VI crowned King of Sicily
v.s: On November 20, Holy Roman Emperor Henry VI (son of Frederick I) was crowned King of Sicily in Palermo. Through his marriage to Constance, daughter of King Roger II of Sicily, he had acquired a claim to the Sicilian throne. However, the Sicilian nobility, opposed to German rule, elected Tancred (Roger II's illegitimate son) as king. Henry VI conquered Sicily in 1194 and executed Tancred. The incorporation of Sicily into the Holy Roman Empire created a pincer movement around the Papal States (Germany to the north, Sicily to the south), arousing the vigilance of the Pope and the Italian city-states.

v.t: Qingyuan Proscription — Han Tuozhou attacks Neo-Confucianism
v.s: To consolidate his power, Han Tuozhou attacked the scholar-official group led by Zhao Ruyu. In 1195, Han declared Zhu Xi, Zhao Ruyu, Lü Zuqian, and 59 others to be members of the 'False Learning Faction', banning them from official positions and destroying their Neo-Confucian works. This became known as the 'Qingyuan Proscription'. Zhu Xi was dismissed from office and returned to his home in Fujian to lecture. Many of his disciples were exiled or imprisoned. The proscription lasted six years (1195–1201) until Han Tuozhou, preparing for a northern expedition, relaxed controls. After the proscription was lifted in 1202, Neo-Confucianism became even more popular.

v.t: Northern Crusade — Livonian Brothers of the Sword conquer Livonia
v.s: In 1195, the German Livonian Brothers of the Sword (formally founded in 1202) launched a crusade in Livonia (modern Latvia and Estonia), conquering the local pagan tribes. The knights forcibly converted the population to Christianity and built Riga Castle (1201). The Sword Brothers later merged with the Teutonic Knights, becoming the dominant power in the Baltic. These Northern Crusades led to massive death and cultural assimilation of the native Baltic peoples.

v.t: Death of Holy Roman Emperor Henry VI
v.s: On September 28, Holy Roman Emperor Henry VI (1165–1197) died of illness (malaria, or possibly poison) in Messina, Sicily, at age 32. Henry VI was the most powerful emperor of the Hohenstaufen dynasty. He had captured King Richard I of England and extorted a huge ransom; he incorporated the Kingdom of Sicily, extending imperial power to the Mediterranean. After his death, his three-year-old son Frederick (later Emperor Frederick II) was left to inherit the throne. The empire plunged into a succession struggle (between the Welf and Hohenstaufen families), and Pope Innocent III took advantage to expand his power.

v.t: Establishment of the Choe military regime in Goryeo
v.s: The Goryeo military officer Choe Chung-heon (1149–1219) staged a coup, killing the powerful minister Yi Ui-min, and took control of the Goryeo government. Choe established the 'Choe regime' (1196–1258), which maintained a military dictatorship for over 60 years. He set up the 'Gyŏngdae Dogam' (Supreme Military Council), which acted above the king. The Choe regime suppressed peasant rebellions (such as the Manjŏk and Mang-i uprisings) and resisted the Mongols. The Choe regime was the longest period of military rule in Korean history.

v.t: Amalric crowned King of Jerusalem
v.s: In 1197, after a struggle over the succession to the Kingdom of Jerusalem, Amalric II (Aimery of Lusignan, brother of the former King Guy of Jerusalem) was crowned king. He was also King of Cyprus (having purchased it from Richard I in 1194). Amalric II concluded a peace agreement with the Ayyubids, and Cyprus became an important crusader base in the East. He died in 1205.

v.t: Emperor Zhangzong of Jin continues sinicization
v.s: During his reign, Emperor Zhangzong of Jin fully adopted Song institutions. In 1197, he ordered the compilation of the Dajin Jili, revising court ceremonies; established the Shangyao Bureau and Tai Hospital, introducing Song medicine; expanded the examination system and increased quotas. Zhangzong also encouraged Jurchen nobles to learn Chinese characters and Confucian classics. His sinicization policies accelerated the Jurchens' assimilation into Chinese culture, but also led to the decline in military effectiveness of the Jurchen Meng'an and Mouke households (they gradually ceased to practice riding and archery).

v.t: Pope Innocent III ascends
v.s: On January 8, Lotario dei Conti di Segni (a cardinal) was elected pope, taking the name Innocent III (1161–1216). He was one of the most influential popes of the Middle Ages. Innocent III asserted that 'the Pope is the sun, the emperor the moon,' claiming supreme authority over both spiritual and temporal powers. During his papacy, the papacy reached its zenith: he forced King John of England, King Philip II of France, and Holy Roman Emperor Otto IV to submit; launched the Fourth Crusade (1202–1204) and the Albigensian Crusade (1209–1229); and approved the founding of the Franciscan and Dominican orders.

v.t: Small-scale border clashes between Southern Song and Jin
v.s: During the Qingyuan Proscription, internal politics of the Southern Song were unstable. The Jin built fortifications on the northern border, and the Song strengthened defense along the Huai River. In 1198, the Jin, using the pretext of 'banditry along the border', attacked the Song outposts at Xuyi and Haozhou. The Song defenders counterattacked, and there were small-scale clashes. After negotiations, the Jin withdrew. This was a minor incident in the long period of Song-Jin peace.

v.t: John succeeds to the English throne
v.s: On April 6, King Richard I of England (the Lionheart) died after being shot by a crossbow bolt while besieging the castle of Châlus-Chabrol in Limousin, France; the wound became infected. Richard had no legitimate children, so his brother John (1166–1216) succeeded, known as 'John Lackland'. At John's accession, most of the Angevin Empire's French territories (Normandy, Anjou, Aquitaine) had already been reconquered by King Philip II of France. John's war with Philip II failed, and in 1204 he lost Normandy. He also came into conflict with Pope Innocent III, leading to England being placed under an interdict (1208–1213). In 1215, John was forced to sign the Magna Carta.

v.t: Qingyuan Proscription loosened in Southern Song
v.s: In order to gain the support of the scholar-officials for his planned northern campaign, Han Tuozhou began to relax the Qingyuan Proscription. In 1199, he allowed exiled officials to be moved to a better location (liangyi), but key figures such as Zhu Xi were still not recalled. The proscription was formally lifted in 1201, and Neo-Confucianism was restored to legal status. Han's preparations for the northern campaign included reappointing hawkish officials and refurbishing warships and weapons.

v.t: Death of Zhu Xi — the great synthesizer of Neo-Confucianism
v.s: On April 23, the Southern Song philosopher and Neo-Confucian master Zhu Xi (1130–1200) died of illness in Jianyang at age 71. At his death, the Qingyuan Proscription had not been fully lifted, and his disciples dared not attend his funeral. Zhu Xi was the great synthesizer of Song-Ming Neo-Confucianism; he established a philosophical system centered on 'principle' (li) with the method of 'investigating things to extend knowledge'. He is revered as 'Master Zhu'. His commentary on the Four Books (Sishu Zhangju Jizhu) became the standard textbook for the imperial examinations in the Yuan, Ming, and Qing dynasties (in 1313, the Yuan court decreed that the examinations should be based on the Four Books). His educational thought (as expressed in the White Deer Grotto Academy Regulations) also had a profound influence.

v.t: University of Paris receives its official charter
v.s: In 1200, King Philip II Augustus of France formally issued a charter recognizing the legal status of the University of Paris. The charter stipulated that teachers and students of the university enjoyed judicial immunity (only the university court could try them), and were exempt from taxes and corvée labor. The University of Paris became the first European university to receive a royal charter. In the same year, Pope Innocent III issued a bull confirming the teaching autonomy of the university's theology faculty. The University of Paris rapidly developed into the academic center of Europe, known as the 'Mother of European Universities'.

v.t: Southward migration of the Tai people — prelude to the Sukhothai Kingdom
v.s: At the beginning of the 13th century, the Tai people migrated south from Yunnan and Guangxi in China, entering the Chao Phraya River valley. They overthrew the rule of the Khmer Empire and in 1238 established the first independent Thai kingdom, the Sukhothai dynasty. Around 1200, the Tai had already founded several small city-states in the upper Chao Phraya valley (such as Sukhothai and Phitsanulok), laying the groundwork for later unification. The southward migration of the Tai peoples changed the ethnic landscape of Southeast Asia.

v.t: Aztec migration — from Aztlán to the Valley of Mexico
v.s: According to Aztec legend, their ancestors migrated south from Aztlán ('Place of the Herons', exact location unknown, possibly in the southwestern US or northern Mexico). Around 1200, they reached the Valley of Mexico and settled near Lake Texcoco. In 1325, they founded Tenochtitlan (modern Mexico City) on an island in the lake. Through military conquests and alliances, the Aztecs gradually became the most powerful empire in Mesoamerica (1428–1521).

v.t: Peak of the Cahokia city-state
v.s: The Mississippian city of Cahokia (modern Illinois) reached its peak around 1200. It was the largest city in North America at the time, with a population of about 15,000–20,000. The city contained more than 120 earthen platform mounds (pyramids), the largest being Monk's Mound (30 m high, with a base larger than the Great Pyramid of Giza). Cahokia was the largest pre-Columbian city north of Mesoamerica. It declined after 1350 due to environmental degradation and population pressure.

v.t: Inca expansion begins — legend of Manco Cápac
v.s: According to Inca legend, the first Inca king, Manco Cápac, established the Kingdom of Cusco around 1200. He and his wife (also his sister) Mama Ocllo emerged from Lake Titicaca in search of a place where a golden rod would sink into the ground, and settled in the Cusco valley. Manco Cápac was considered the son of the sun god Inti. Despite the legendary nature, archaeological evidence indicates that the Cusco valley indeed saw the emergence of a city-state in the early 13th century. In 1438, Pachacuti expanded the kingdom into the Inca Empire.

v.t: Flourishing of Benin bronze art
v.s: From about 1200 to 1897, the Benin Kingdom in Nigeria was famous for its court bronzes and brass reliefs. Beninese artists used the lost-wax method to create elaborate heads of kings and carved plaques depicting court scenes. The city of Benin built a wall system about 5 km long (considered one of the largest man-made earthworks in the world). The kingdom engaged in the trade of pepper, ivory, and slaves, and maintained about four centuries of prosperity after contact with European merchants.

v.t: Polynesian settlement of New Zealand
v.s: Around 1200–1300, Polynesian voyagers from the Cook or Society Islands reached New Zealand (Aotearoa) in double-hulled canoes. They became the ancestors of the Māori. The Māori preserved this history through oral genealogies and the legend of Kupe's discovery. The Māori developed distinctive wood carving, tā moko (tattoo), and tribal (iwi) systems, and found greenstone (pounamu) in the South Island. The giant moa bird became a key food source.

v.t: Manco Cápac founds the Inca Empire (legendary)
v.s: According to Inca tradition, Manco Cápac and his sister-wife Mama Ocllo emerged from Lake Titicaca and founded the city of Cusco, the capital of the Inca Empire. This legendary origin marks the beginning of Inca civilization in the Andes. The historical accuracy remains debated, but the narrative served as the foundational myth of Tahuantinsuyu.

v.t: Samoan-Tongan Empire controls the Pacific
v.s: Around 1200 AD, the Tu'i Tonga Empire reached its zenith, dominating the Pacific islands including Samoa, Fiji, and parts of Micronesia. The Tongan navy controlled maritime trade routes, and tribute flowed from surrounding islands to the Tongan capital. This maritime empire represented one of the most extensive seafaring civilizations in the Pacific, with influence stretching over 3,000 kilometers of ocean.
